```js
public addTrack(track: TrackOptions) {
  let index = this.tracks.findIndex((t) => t.id === track.id)
  if (index !== -1) {
    this.tracks[index] = track  // Replace the whole track
    this.initAudio(track).then((audio) => {
      this.audios[index] = audio
      // Recreate wavesurfer
    })
  }
}
```

```js
private initAudio(track: TrackOptions): Promise<HTMLAudioElement | WebAudioPlayer> {
  if (track.anchoredFrame !== undefined && track.anchoredFrame !== 0) {

    audio = new WebAudioPlayer(this.audioContext, { headLength: 0 });
  }
```

```
1: /**
2:  * AudioWorklet processor for recording microphone audio
3:  * Uses AudioContext's clock (currentTime) for sample-accurate timing
4:  * Share the same clock as playback - symmetric jitter for better sync
5:  * Maintains a 2-second rolling buffer for pre-roll support
6:  */
7: class RecorderWorkletProcessor extends AudioWorkletProcessor {
8:   static get parameterDescriptors() {
9:     return [{
10:       name: 'isRecording',
11:       defaultValue: 0,
12:       minValue: 0,
13:       maxValue: 1,
14:     }];
15:   }
16: 
17:   constructor() {
18:     super();
19:     this._isRecording = false;
20:     this._audioData = []; // Array of Float32Array for current recording
21: 
22:     // Get headLength from processor options (passed from RecordingEngine)
23:     let headLength = 1.0;
24:     // Access processorOptions directly - no TypeScript syntax
25:     if (this.processorOptions && this.processorOptions.headLength !== undefined) {
26:       headLength = this.processorOptions.headLength;
27:     }
28:     // Rolling buffer should always be larger than headLength to work properly
29:     // Use at least 2 seconds or headLength + 1 second buffer
30:     this._rollingBufferDuration = Math.max(2.0, headLength + 1.0);
31:     this._recordingStartFrame = 0; // REAL TIME frame when recording started
32:     this._sampleRate = sampleRate;
33:     this._processCount = 0;
34:     this._shouldStop = false;
35:     this._anchoredFrame = 0; // Shared-clock frame of punch-in point
36: 
37:     this._rollingBuffer = []; // Initialize rolling buffer
38: 
39:     this.port.onmessage = (event) => {
40:       if (event.data.type === 'STOP_RECORDING') {
41:         this._shouldStop = true;
42:         this.port.postMessage({
43:           type: 'DEBUG',
44:           msg: 'STOP_RECORDING flag set',
45:         });
46:       } else if (event.data.type === 'START_RECORDING') {
47:         this._shouldStop = false;
48: 
49:         if (event.data.punchInUserTime_Real !== undefined) {
50:           this._anchoredFrame = Math.floor(event.data.punchInUserTime_Real * sampleRate);
51: 
52:           // FIXED: Start recording immediately, don't wait for currentTime
53:           // This avoids missed starts when AudioContext is transitioning from suspended
54:           this._isRecording = true;
55:           this._audioData = [];
56:           this._recordingStartFrame = currentFrame;
57: 
58:           this.port.postMessage({
59:             type: 'RECORDING_STARTED',
60:             startTime: this._recordingStartFrame / sampleRate,
61:             anchoredFrame: this._anchoredFrame,
62:             targetPlaybackReal: this._targetPlaybackReal,
63:             currentTime: currentTime,
64:             msg: 'Recording started at currentFrame=' + currentFrame + ', anchoredFrame=' + this._anchoredFrame,
65:           });
66:         }
67:       }
68:     };
69:   }
70: 
71:   process(inputs, outputs, parameters) {
72:     this._processCount++;
73: 
74:     // Handle stop flag from messages
75:     if (this._shouldStop) {
76:       this._shouldStop = false;
77:       if (this._isRecording) {
78:         this._isRecording = false;
79:         const flushed = this._flush();
80:         // Send recorded data back to main thread
81:         this.port.postMessage({
82:           type: 'RECORDING_STOPPED',
83:           audioData: flushed[0],
84:           anchoredFrame: flushed[1],
85:           rollingOffset: flushed[2],
86:         });
87:       }
88:     }
89: 
90:     // Always capture to rolling buffer (when playing)
91:     if (inputs && inputs[0] && inputs[0][0]) {
92:       const channelData = inputs[0][0]; // First channel (mono)
93:       const copy = new Float32Array(channelData.length);
94:       copy.set(channelData);
95: 
96:       // Add to rolling buffer
97:       this._rollingBuffer.push({
98:         data: copy,
99:         frame: currentFrame,
100:       });
101: 
102:       // Trim old data (keep rollingBufferDuration seconds)
103:       const maxFrames = this._rollingBufferDuration * sampleRate;
104:       while (this._rollingBuffer.length > 0) {
105:         if (currentFrame - this._rollingBuffer[0].frame > maxFrames) {
106:           this._rollingBuffer.shift();
107:         } else {
108:           break;
109:         }
110:       }
111:     }
112: 
113:     // If recording, store in _audioData
114:     if (this._isRecording && inputs && inputs[0] && inputs[0][0]) {
115:       const channelData = inputs[0][0];
116:       const copy = new Float32Array(channelData.length);
117:       copy.set(channelData);
118:       this._audioData.push({ data: copy, frame: currentFrame });
119:     }
120: 
121:     return true;
122:   }
123: 
124:   _flush() {
125:     // Combine the rolling buffer (pre-roll) with the recorded data
126:     // The rolling buffer provides the "head" audio before the punch-in point
127:     const allChunks = [];
128:     let rollingOffset = 0;
129: 
130:     if (this._rollingBuffer.length > 0) {
131:       // Find the frame where _recordingStartFrame begins in the rolling buffer
132:       const recordStartFrame = this._recordingStartFrame;
133: 
134:       // Include rolling buffer data up to recording start
135:       for (let i = 0; i < this._rollingBuffer.length; i++) {
136:         const chunk = this._rollingBuffer[i];
137:         const chunkEndFrame = chunk.frame + chunk.data.length;
138: 
139:         if (chunkEndFrame <= recordStartFrame) {
140:           // Entirely before recording start — include as head
141:           allChunks.push(chunk.data);
142:           rollingOffset += chunk.data.length;
143:         } else if (chunk.frame < recordStartFrame) {
144:           // Partially overlaps — only include the pre-recording portion
145:           const offset = recordStartFrame - chunk.frame;
146:           if (offset > 0 && offset < chunk.data.length) {
147:             allChunks.push(chunk.data.subarray(0, offset));
148:             rollingOffset += offset;
149:           }
150:           break;
151:         } else {
152:           break;
153:         }
154:       }
155:     }
156: 
157:     // Append the recorded audio data
158:     if (this._audioData.length > 0) {
159:       const totalRecordingLength = this._audioData.reduce((sum, chunk) => sum + chunk.data.length, 0);
160:       const combinedRecording = new Float32Array(totalRecordingLength);
161:       let offset = 0;
162:       this._audioData.forEach(chunk => {
163:         combinedRecording.set(chunk.data, offset);
164:         offset += chunk.data.length;
165:       });
166:       allChunks.push(combinedRecording);
167:     }
168: 
169:     // Combine everything into one array
170:     let totalLength = 0;
171:     allChunks.forEach(c => totalLength += c.length);
172:     const result = new Float32Array(totalLength);
173:     let offset = 0;
174:     allChunks.forEach(c => { result.set(c, offset); offset += c.length; });
175: 
176:     // FIXED: Return _recordingStartFrame instead of _performanceStartFrame
177:     // _recordingStartFrame is set to currentFrame when recording starts in process()
178:     // This gives us the exact shared-clock frame where audio capture began
179:     const returnFrame = this._recordingStartFrame;
180:     this.port.postMessage({
181:       type: 'DEBUG',
182:       msg: '_flush() returning',
183:       recordingStartFrame: returnFrame,
184:       anchoredFrame: this._anchoredFrame,
185:       rollingOffset: rollingOffset,
186:       audioDataLength: result.length,
187:       sampleRate: sampleRate,
188:     });
189:     this._audioData = [];
190:     return [result, returnFrame, rollingOffset];
191:   }
192: }
193: 
194: registerProcessor('recorder-worklet', RecorderWorkletProcessor);
```

```
<type>file</type>
<content>
1: /**
2:  * AudioWorklet processor for recording microphone audio
3:  * Uses AudioContext's clock (currentTime) for sample-accurate timing
4:  * Share the same clock as playback - symmetric jitter for better sync
5:  * Maintains a 2-second rolling buffer for pre-roll support
6:  */
7: class RecorderWorkletProcessor extends AudioWorkletProcessor {
8:   static get parameterDescriptors() {
9:     return [{
10:       name: 'isRecording',
11:       defaultValue: 0,
12:       minValue: 0,
13:       maxValue: 1,
14:     }];
15:   }
16: 
17:   constructor() {
18:     super();
19:     this._isRecording = false;
20:     this._audioData = []; // Array of Float32Array for current recording
21: 
22:     // Get headLength from processor options (passed from RecordingEngine)
23:     let headLength = 1.0;
24:     // Access processorOptions directly - no TypeScript syntax
25:     if (this.processorOptions && this.processorOptions.headLength !== undefined) {
26:       headLength = this.processorOptions.headLength;
27:     }
28:     // Rolling buffer should always be larger than headLength to work properly
29:     // Use at least 2 seconds or headLength + 1 second buffer
30:     this._rollingBufferDuration = Math.max(2.0, headLength + 1.0);
31:     this._recordingStartFrame = 0; // REAL TIME frame when recording started
32:     this._sampleRate = sampleRate;
33:     this._processCount = 0;
34:     this._shouldStop = false;
35:     this._anchoredFrame = 0; // Shared-clock frame of punch-in point
36: 
37:     this._rollingBuffer = []; // Initialize rolling buffer
38: 
39:     this.port.onmessage = (event) => {
40:       if (event.data.type === 'STOP_RECORDING') {
41:         this._shouldStop = true;
42:         this.port.postMessage({
43:           type: 'DEBUG',
44:           msg: 'STOP_RECORDING flag set',
45:         });
46:       } else if (event.data.type === 'START_RECORDING') {
47:         this._shouldStop = false;
48: 
49:         if (event.data.punchInUserTime_Real !== undefined) {
50:           this._anchoredFrame = Math.floor(event.data.punchInUserTime_Real * sampleRate);
51: 
52:           // FIXED: Start recording immediately, don't wait for currentTime
53:           // This avoids missed starts when AudioContext is transitioning from suspended
54:           this._isRecording = true;
55:           this._audioData = [];
56:           this._recordingStartFrame = currentFrame;
57: 
58:           this.port.postMessage({
59:             type: 'RECORDING_STARTED',
60:             startTime: this._recordingStartFrame / sampleRate,
61:             anchoredFrame: this._anchoredFrame,
62:             currentTime: currentTime,
63:             msg: 'Recording started at currentFrame=' + currentFrame + ', anchoredFrame=' + this._anchoredFrame,
64:           });
65:         }
66:       }
67:     };
68:   }
69: 
70:   process(inputs, outputs, parameters) {
71:     this._processCount++;
72: 
73:     // Handle stop flag from messages
74:     if (this._shouldStop) {
75:       this._shouldStop = false;
76:       if (this._isRecording) {
77:         this._isRecording = false;
78:         const flushed = this._flush();
79:         // Send recorded data back to main thread
80:         this.port.postMessage({
81:           type: 'RECORDING_STOPPED',
82:           audioData: flushed[0],
83:           anchoredFrame: flushed[1],
84:           rollingOffset: flushed[2],
85:         });
86:       }
87:     }
88: 
89:     // Always capture to rolling buffer (when playing)
90:     if (inputs && inputs[0] && inputs[0][0]) {
91:       const channelData = inputs[0][0]; // First channel (mono)
92:       const copy = new Float32Array(channelData.length);
93:       copy.set(channelData);
94: 
95:       // Add to rolling buffer
96:       this._rollingBuffer.push({
97:         data: copy,
98:         frame: currentFrame,
99:       });
100: 
101:       // Trim old data (keep rollingBufferDuration seconds)
102:       const maxFrames = this._rollingBufferDuration * sampleRate;
103:       while (this._rollingBuffer.length > 0) {
104:         if (currentFrame - this._rollingBuffer[0].frame > maxFrames) {
105:           this._rollingBuffer.shift();
106:         } else {
107:           break;
108:         }
109:       }
110:     }
111: 
112:     // If recording, store in _audioData
113:     if (this._isRecording && inputs && inputs[0] && inputs[0][0]) {
114:       const channelData = inputs[0][0];
115:       const copy = new Float32Array(channelData.length);
116:       copy.set(channelData);
117:       this._audioData.push({ data: copy, frame: currentFrame });
118:     }
119: 
120:     return true;
121:   }
122: 
123:   _flush() {
124:     // Combine the rolling buffer (pre-roll) with the recorded data
125:     // The rolling buffer provides the "head" audio before the punch-in point
126:     const allChunks = [];
127:     let rollingOffset = 0;
128: 
129:     if (this._rollingBuffer.length > 0) {
130:       // Find the frame where _recordingStartFrame begins in the rolling buffer
131:       const recordStartFrame = this._recordingStartFrame;
132: 
133:       // Include rolling buffer data up to recording start
134:       for (let i = 0; i < this._rollingBuffer.length; i++) {
135:         const chunk = this._rollingBuffer[i];
136:         const chunkEndFrame = chunk.frame + chunk.data.length;
137: 
138:         if (chunkEndFrame <= recordStartFrame) {
139:           // Entirely before recording start — include as head
140:           allChunks.push(chunk.data);
141:           rollingOffset += chunk.data.length;
142:         } else if (chunk.frame < recordStartFrame) {
143:           // Partially overlaps — only include the pre-recording portion
144:           const offset = recordStartFrame - chunk.frame;
145:           if (offset > 0 && offset < chunk.data.length) {
146:             allChunks.push(chunk.data.subarray(0, offset));
147:             rollingOffset += offset;
148:           }
149:           break;
150:         } else {
151:           break;
152:         }
153:       }
154:     }
155: 
156:     // Append the recorded audio data
157:     if (this._audioData.length > 0) {
158:       const totalRecordingLength = this._audioData.reduce((sum, chunk) => sum + chunk.data.length, 0);
159:       const combinedRecording = new Float32Array(totalRecordingLength);
160:       let offset = 0;
161:       this._audioData.forEach(chunk => {
162:         combinedRecording.set(chunk.data, offset);
163:         offset += chunk.data.length;
164:       });
165:       allChunks.push(combinedRecording);
166:     }
167: 
168:     // Combine everything into one array
169:     let totalLength = 0;
170:     allChunks.forEach(c => totalLength += c.length);
171:     const result = new Float32Array(totalLength);
172:     let offset = 0;
173:     allChunks.forEach(c => { result.set(c, offset); offset += c.length; });
174: 
175:     // FIXED: Return _recordingStartFrame instead of _performanceStartFrame
176:     // _recordingStartFrame is set to currentFrame when recording starts in process()
177:     // This gives us the exact shared-clock frame where audio capture began
178:     const returnFrame = this._recordingStartFrame;
179:     this.port.postMessage({
180:       type: 'DEBUG',
181:       msg: '_flush() returning',
182:       recordingStartFrame: returnFrame,
183:       anchoredFrame: this._anchoredFrame,
184:       rollingOffset: rollingOffset,
185:       audioDataLength: result.length,
186:       sampleRate: sampleRate,
187:     });
188:     this._audioData = [];
189:     return [result, returnFrame, rollingOffset];
190:   }
191: }
192: 
193: registerProcessor('recorder-worklet', RecorderWorkletProcessor);

(End of file - total 193 lines)
</content>
```

```
-    if (this._audioData.length === 0) return [new Float32Array(0), 0];
-    
-    // Combine all chunks into one Float32Array (NO filtering - keep all audio)
-    const totalLength = this._audioData.reduce((sum, chunk) => sum + chunk.data.length, 0);
-    const combinedData = new Float32Array(totalLength);
+    // Combine the rolling buffer (pre-roll) with the recorded data
+    // The rolling buffer provides the "head" audio before the punch-in point
+    const allChunks = [];
+    let rollingOffset = 0;
+
+    if (this._rollingBuffer.length > 0) {
+      // Find the frame where _recordingStartFrame begins in the rolling buffer
+      const recordStartFrame = this._recordingStartFrame;
+
+      // Include rolling buffer data up to recording start
+      for (let i = 0; i < this._rollingBuffer.length; i++) {
+        const chunk = this._rollingBuffer[i];
+        const chunkEndFrame = chunk.frame + chunk.data.length;
+
+        if (chunkEndFrame <= recordStartFrame) {
+          // Entirely before recording start — include as head
+          allChunks.push(chunk.data);
+          rollingOffset += chunk.data.length;
+        } else if (chunk.frame < recordStartFrame) {
+          // Partially overlaps — only include the pre-recording portion
+          const offset = recordStartFrame - chunk.frame;
+          if (offset > 0 && offset < chunk.data.length) {
+            allChunks.push(chunk.data.subarray(0, offset));
+            rollingOffset += offset;
+          }
+          break;
+        } else {
+          break;
+        }
+      }
+    }
+
+    // Append the recorded audio data
+    if (this._audioData.length > 0) {
+      const totalRecordingLength = this._audioData.reduce((sum, chunk) => sum + chunk.data.length, 0);
+      const combinedRecording = new Float32Array(totalRecordingLength);
+      let offset = 0;
+      this._audioData.forEach(chunk => {
+        combinedRecording.set(chunk.data, offset);
+        offset += chunk.data.length;
+      });
+      allChunks.push(combinedRecording);
+    }
+
+    // Combine everything into one array
+    let totalLength = 0;
+    allChunks.forEach(c => totalLength += c.length);
+    const result = new Float32Array(totalLength);
     let offset = 0;
-    
-    this._audioData.forEach(chunk => {
-      combinedData.set(chunk.data, offset);
-      offset += chunk.data.length;
+    allChunks.forEach(c => { result.set(c, offset); offset += c.length; });
+
+    // FIXED: Return _recordingStartFrame instead of _performanceStartFrame
+    // _recordingStartFrame is set to currentFrame when recording starts in process()
+    // This gives us the exact shared-clock frame where audio capture began
+    const returnFrame = this._recordingStartFrame;
+    this.port.postMessage({
+      type: 'DEBUG',
+      msg: '_flush() returning',
+      recordingStartFrame: returnFrame,
+      anchoredFrame: this._anchoredFrame,
+      rollingOffset: rollingOffset,
+      audioDataLength: result.length,
+      sampleRate: sampleRate,
     });
-    
     this._audioData = [];
-    // Return performanceStartFrame (when punchInUserTime was reached)
-    // This is the sync point for playback
-    return [combinedData, this._performanceStartFrame];
+    return [result, returnFrame, rollingOffset];
   }
```

The user is reporting a new issue: some recorded clips don't play back audio — they appear visually but there's no sound when the playhead hovers over them. This is in PLAN MODE (read-only), so I need to analyze and plan, not implement.

Let me think about what could cause this issue given the codebase and the changes we've been making.

Looking at the flow:
1. After recording, `handleAudioWorkletStop` creates an AudioBuffer and WAV blob
2. A URL is created from the WAV blob: `URL.createObjectURL(wavBlob)`
3. This URL is passed to the store via `onAddPhrase`
4. The multitrack system loads this URL and creates a WebAudioPlayer
5. During playback, `updatePosition` calculates `newTime = time - track.startPosition` and calls `audio.currentTime` and `audio.playAt()`

Potential causes for "visually there but no audio":

1. **`updatePosition` never reaches the clip's time range**: If `track.startPosition` is stored incorrectly (e.g., still UserTime instead of RealTime after our partial fixes), then `newTime = time - track.startPosition` could be wrong. If the clip's startPosition is negative (like -3), then `newTime` would be larger than expected, potentially pushing it past the duration.

2. **WebAudioPlayer gain node muted/disconnected**: The muted setter has logic that disconnects/reconnects the gain node. If there's a state where it's disconnected and never reconnected, audio would be silent.

3. **The blob URL is created but the audio buffer fails to decode**: The WAV encoding might produce invalid data.

4. **`updatePosition` precision check prevents playback**: Line 455 has `Math.abs(audio.currentTime - newTime) > precisionSeconds` — if the difference is within 0.05s, it won't seek, and the clip might not start playing.

5. **The `isPaused` flag in WebAudioPlayer**: If a previous playback left the player in a state where `paused` is false but the bufferNode is stopped, subsequent play attempts might be skipped because `playAt` has `if (!this.paused) return`.

6. **`updatePosition` only plays when `!isPaused && audio.paused`**: It checks `if (!isPaused && audio.paused)` to start playback. But if the audio is already "not paused" (because it was previously played and never properly stopped), it won't re-trigger `playAt`. The `currentTime` sync at line 475-478 would try to update `audio.currentTime` but for WebAudioPlayer, setting `currentTime` while playing would first pause, set the time, then play — which could cause issues.

7. **Clip duration vs track duration mismatch**: In `updatePosition`, `duration` comes from `this.durations[index]`, which is set during `initAllAudios`. If this doesn't match the actual audio buffer duration, the bounds check could prevent playback.

8. **The `newTime` calculation**: `const newTime = time - track.startPosition`. If `track.startPosition` is the RealTime position and `time` is the real-time position from the sync loop, this should give the local position within the clip. But if there's a mismatch...

But in `updatePosition`, `time` comes from the sync loop which uses RealTime. So `newTime = realTime - (-1) = realTime + 1`. Actually wait, let me re-examine something. When `addTrack` is called from `onAddPhrase`, the track's phrase already has the correct data. This could be problematic if both happen quickly.

But with the `syncedTrackIdsRef` guard, the App.tsx effect won't call `addTrack` for tracks that have already been synced. So:
1. Recording ends → `onAddPhrase` → `addTrack` → syncedTrackIdsRef adds track.id
2. Re-render → App.tsx effect fires → sees track.id already in syncedTrackIdsRef → skips

```js
} else if (newTime >= 0 && Math.abs(audio.currentTime - newTime) > precisionSeconds) {

```

This should work. But if the drift is less than `precisionSeconds` (0.05s), no correction happens — which is fine, tiny drift is OK.

**Hypothesis 2: The audio buffer is created but empty**

In `handleAudioWorkletStop`, the AudioBuffer is created from the Float32Array. If the data is all zeros or very quiet, it would appear to have no audio. But the duration would still be correct, so visually the clip would show up.

**Hypothesis 3: The WebAudioPlayer's gainNode is muted**

In `muted` setter:
```js
set muted(value: boolean) {
    if (this._muted === value) return
    this._muted = value
    if (this._muted) {
        this.gainNode.disconnect()
    } else {
        if (!this.gainNode.context?.destination) {
            this.gainNode.connect(this.audioContext.destination)
        }
    }
}
```

The `loadBuffer` method sets up the buffer but doesn't check muted state. The `setBuffer` method calls `play()` if `autoplay` is true, but `autoplay` is never set to true in the multitrack code.

Actually wait — when `initAudio` is called for a recorded clip, it creates a WebAudioPlayer but doesn't set it to autoplay. The `loadBuffer` method triggers `play()` only if `autoplay` is true. So the audio is loaded but not playing.

Then in `updatePosition`, during the initial call, `this._isPlaying` might be false (multitrack hasn't started playing yet). So the code enters the `if (isPaused || newTime > duration)` branch and calls `audio.pause()` (which is a no-op since it's already paused) and optionally sets `audio.currentTime`.

When multitrack later starts playing (via `multitrack.play()`), it calls `startSync` which uses requestAnimationFrame. On the next frame, `updatePosition` is called with `isPaused = false` and `audio.paused = true`, so it calls `webaudio.playAt(startTime)`. This should work.

**Hypothesis 4: The `_flush()` return value issue (our bug #1)**

Before our fix, `anchoredFrame` was always `undefined`. The RecordingEngine had:
```js
const anchorFrame = anchoredFrame ?? 0;
const startPos = (anchorFrame / sampleRate) - secondsPerBar - this.headLength;
```

With `anchorFrame = 0`, `startPos = 0/sr - 2 - 1 = -3` (for 2s bar). This negative startPosition means the clip is placed before the start of Bar 1.

In multitrack, `updatePosition` calculates `newTime = time - track.startPosition`. If `track.startPosition` is -3, then `newTime = time + 3`. For the clip to be visible, `time` must be >= -3 and the clip duration reaches to about 3.6s (`time` ≈ -3 + 6.59 = 3.59). So the clip is visible from RealTime -3s to about 3.6s.

But when playback starts at RealTime 0 (Bar 1), `newTime = 0 - (-3) = 3`. The WebAudioPlayer would try to play from offset 3s into a 6.59s buffer. That should still produce audio...

Wait, but there's a subtlety. The `audio.currentTime` setter and `playAt` method:
- `audio.currentTime = Math.max(0, newTime)` → sets `playedDuration = 3`
- Then `webaudio.playAt(startTime)` → starts playing from offset 3

This should work. The audio should play from 3s into the buffer, through the end.

**Hypothesis 5: addTrack during playback causes issues**

If the user records, then immediately plays, the `addTrack` from `onAddPhrase` happens asynchronously (after the WASM decode). The track might be fully decoded and added while the multitrack is already playing. The `updatePosition` loop would then find the new audio track and should start it...

Given the complexity, I think the most likely causes based on the code are:

**A. AudioContext state issue**: If the AudioContext was suspended (e.g., due to user gesture requirements), the WebAudioPlayer's gainNode might be connected but no actual audio passes through. The `playAt` method uses `this.audioContext.currentTime` for scheduling, which doesn't advance when suspended.

**B. Playback restart after addTrack during playing**: If `addTrack` is called while playing, it destroys the old audio and creates new. But the `updatePosition` might not pick up the new audio immediately because of how the play state check works.

**C. The `playedDuration` issue with WebAudioPlayer**: When `set currentTime(value)` is called on a paused WebAudioPlayer, it sets `playedDuration = value`. When `play()` or `playAt()` is next called, it uses `playedDuration` as the offset. But if `currentTime` was set to a value that exceeds the buffer duration, the check in `playAt` would emit 'ended' instead of playing.

Let me look at the specific flow:

1. Recording produces a clip of 6.6s duration
2. `startPosition` is -3 (before fix) or correct value (after fix)
3. User starts playback, multitrack `updatePosition` runs
4. When `time` (RealTime) enters the clip's range, `newTime = time - track.startPosition` 
5. For pre-fix: `newTime = 0 - (-3) = 3` — plays from 3s into the 6.6s buffer — should work
6. For post-fix: `startPosition = anchorFrame/sr - sPB - HL = actualValue - 2 - 1`. If anchor was correctly set, startPosition should be something like -2.99 (close to -3), so behavior would be similar

Wait, maybe I should focus on what the user is actually experiencing. They say "some recorded clips don't play back." Given the current codebase (with bugs partially fixed), what scenarios could cause this?

Actually, I just realized I might be overthinking this. Let me look at a very specific issue:

In the `updatePosition` method, there's this code for seeking:
```js
if (!isPaused && audio.paused) {
    const webaudio = audio as WebAudioPlayer;
    if (typeof webaudio.playAt === 'function' && this.audioContext) {
        audio.currentTime = Math.max(0, newTime);
```

Setting `audio.currentTime` on a WebAudioPlayer triggers the setter which:
```js
set currentTime(value) {
    this.emitEvent('seeking')
    if (this.paused) {
        this.playedDuration = value
    } else {
        this.pause()
        this.playedDuration = value
        this.play()
    }
    this.emitEvent('timeupdate')
}
```

Since the audio is paused (we just checked), `playedDuration = value`. Good.

Then `playAt(startTime)` is called:
```js
async playAt(startTime: number) {
    if (!this.paused) return
    this.paused = false
    this.bufferNode?.disconnect()
    this.bufferNode = this.audioContext.createBufferSource()
    this.bufferNode.buffer = this.buffer
    ...
    this.bufferNode.start(startTime, offset)
    this.playStartTime = startTime
}
```

This should work IF the buffer is properly set. But here's a subtle issue: `this.buffer` could be `null`. When would this happen?

In `loadBuffer`:
```js
loadBuffer(url: string, audioBuffer: AudioBuffer) {
    this._src = url
    this.setBuffer(audioBuffer)
}
```

And `setBuffer`:
```js
setBuffer(audioBuffer: AudioBuffer) {
    this.buffer = audioBuffer
    this._duration = audioBuffer.duration
    this.emitEvent('loadedmetadata')
    this.emitEvent('canplay')
    if (this.autoplay) {
        this.play()
    }
}
```

So `buffer` should be set. But what about the case where `src` is used instead of `loadBuffer`? Then:
```js
set src(value: string) {
    ...
    fetch(value)
        .then(...)
        .then((audioBuffer) => {
            ...
            this.buffer = audioBuffer
            ...
        })
}
```

This is async. If `updatePosition` is called before the fetch completes, `this.buffer` is null, and `playAt` would hit the `else if (this._src)` branch with `console.warn('WebAudioPlayer: No buffer to play')`.

This is particularly relevant for the `addTrack` flow:
1. `addTrack` is called
2. `initAudio` creates a WebAudioPlayer and loads the URL
3. The promise resolves when the audio is loaded
4. Then `addTrack` continues with the `then` callback

But here's the issue: even after the promise resolves (metadata loaded), the actual `fetch` + `decodeAudioData` might still be in progress for the `src` setter path. The promise resolves via `loadedmetadata` event, but the buffer might not be decoded yet.

Wait, actually let me re-read `initAudio`:

```js
if (track.audioBuffer && audio instanceof WebAudioPlayer && track.url) {
    audio.loadBuffer(track.url, track.audioBuffer)  // <-- synchronous buffer set + metadata emit
} else if (track.url) {
    audio.src = track.url  // <-- async fetch and decode
}
```

For the `addTrack` case from `onAddPhrase`, the `trackOptions` doesn't have `audioBuffer` at the top level, so it falls to `audio.src = track.url` which is async. Then the promise resolves on `loadedmetadata` event... but with the `src` setter, that event fires AFTER decode:

```js
.then((audioBuffer) => {
    if (this.src !== value || !audioBuffer) return null
    this.buffer = audioBuffer
    this._duration = audioBuffer.duration
    this.emitEvent('loadedmetadata')
    this.emitEvent('canplay')
    if (this.autoplay) {
        this.play()
    }
})
```

So `loadedmetadata` fires after the buffer is set. Good.

But the initial promise resolution in `initAudio` is:
```js
return new Promise<...>((resolve) => {
    if (!audio.src) return resolve(audio)
    if (track.audioBuffer && audio instanceof WebAudioPlayer) return resolve(audio)
    ;(audio as HTMLAudioElement).addEventListener('loadedmetadata', () => resolve(audio), { once: true })
})
```

For the `src` path, it waits for `loadedmetadata`. For the `loadBuffer` path, it returns immediately (because `!audio.src` is true... wait, let me check. After `loadBuffer`, `this._src = url`, so `!audio.src` is FALSE. Then it checks `track.audioBuffer && audio instanceof WebAudioPlayer` — this IS true for the loadBuffer path... NO, wait. In `addTrack`, `audioBuffer` is NOT on the top-level trackOptions.

For tracks with NO phrases, it creates an entry with `id: t.id` (e.g., '1'). For tracks WITH phrases, it creates entries with `id: p.id` (phrase IDs) and `trackId: t.id`.

So initially, track '1' has an entry with `id: '1'` and `url: SILENT_WAV`. When recording happens on track '1', `onAddPhrase` calls `addTrack` with `id: '1'`. The `findIndex` finds the entry at the position where `id === '1'`. It replaces it!

So for the FIRST recording on track 1, it DOES find and replace the placeholder. The new `trackOptions` has `id: '1'`, `url: undefined` (no url at top level), `phrases: [{ url: 'blob:...', audioBuffer: ... }]`.

Then `initAudio(track)` is called. `track.audioBuffer` is undefined (at top level). `track.url` is undefined. So NO buffer is loaded. The Promise resolves immediately. The track's duration is 0.

This means when `updatePosition` runs, it sees duration=0, and the clip effectively plays nothing. The waveform shows because WaveSurfer has the peaks data, but no audio comes out because the WebAudioPlayer has no buffer.

After the WebAudioPlayer is created, `audio.src` is '' (empty string). After `loadBuffer`, `this._src = url` is set. But since neither `loadBuffer` nor `src =` is called (because `track.audioBuffer` is undefined at top level and `track.url` is undefined), `audio.src` remains '', and the promise resolves immediately.

So YES, the bug is in `addTrack` from `onAddPhrase` — the audio data is nested inside `phrases` and not at the top level of the trackOptions object. The `initAudio` function can't find it.

OK so now I understand the root cause. Let me formulate a plan.

**Root Cause (for "no audio on recorded clips"):**

The `addTrack` method expects a flat `TrackOptions` with `url` and `audioBuffer` at the top level. But `onAddPhrase` constructs the trackOptions with audio data nested inside `phrases[0].audioBuffer` and `phrases[0].url`. So `initAudio` finds no data to load, creates a player with an empty buffer, and the track has zero duration.

**Fix:**
In `onAddPhrase`'s `addTrack` call, extract the first phrase's audio data to the top level:

```js
const firstPhrase = track.phrases[0]; // The newly added phrase
const trackOptions = {
    id: track.id,
    name: track.name,
    ...
    url: firstPhrase.url,
    audioBuffer: firstPhrase.audioBuffer,
    peaks: firstPhrase.peaks,
    ...
    // OR, if there could be multiple phrases, use the appropriate one
};
```

Wait, but the multitrack structure allows multiple phrases per track in the `phrases` array. However, `initAudio` only loads ONE audio buffer per track (from `track.url` and `track.audioBuffer`). The multitrack was originally designed with one audio per "track" — each "track" in the multitrack corresponds to one phrase.

So actually, the `addTrack` call from `onAddPhrase` is fundamentally wrong in its structure. It should be passing the NEW PHRASE as a flat track at the correct index, not as a container with nested phrases.

Let me reconsider. The multitrack constructor creates one entry per phrase (each with `trackId` pointing to the parent track). So `this.tracks` in multitrack contains entries like:
- `{ id: 'placeholder', ... }`
- `{ id: 'metronome', trackId: 'metronome', url: '...', ... }`
- `{ id: 'abc123', trackId: '1', url: 'blob:...', startPosition: ..., ... }`
- `{ id: 'def456', trackId: 'metronome', url: 'blob:...', startPosition: 0, ... }`

Where 'abc123' is a phrase ID.

When `addTrack` is called with `id: '1'` (a track ID, not a phrase ID), it looks for a track with that id. It finds the placeholder entry `(id: '1')` if the track had no phrases, or it doesn't find anything if the track already has phrases (because the phrase entries have phrase IDs, not track IDs).

This is getting complicated. Let me re-examine what's actually going on with the data structures.

Looking at `useAudioEngine.ts` more carefully at the multitrack construction:

```js
(tracks || []).forEach(t => {
    const isMetronome = t.id === 'metronome';
    if (t.phrases.length === 0) {
        multitrackItems.push({
            id: t.id,      // '1', '2', etc. for empty tracks
            trackId: t.id,

        });
    } else {
        t.phrases.forEach(p => {
            multitrackItems.push({

                trackId: t.id,   // '1', '2', etc.
                url: p.url,
                audioBuffer: p.audioBuffer,
                ...
            });
        });
    }
});
```

So for track '1' with one phrase, the multitrack has entries:
- Was initially: `{ id: '1', url: SILENT_WAV, ... }` (placeholder for empty track)
- After first recording: replaced by `{ id: 'phrase_id', trackId: '1', url: blob, ... }`

No wait — initially the multitrack is built once. Then when recording adds a phrase, `addTrack` is supposed to update it. Let me trace the actual flow:

1. App starts, store has 6 tracks (metronome + 5), all with empty phrases
2. Multitrack constructor creates 7 entries (1 placeholder + 6 empty track placeholders with SILENT_WAV)
3. Each entry has `id: t.id` (e.g., '1', '2', etc.)
4. User records on track 1
5. `onAddPhrase` fires → `addPhrase` adds phrase to track 1 in store
6. Then `addTrack` is called with trackOptions:
   - `id: '1'` (the track ID)
   - `phrases: [{ url: blob, audioBuffer: buffer, ... }]`
7. In multitrack, `findIndex(t => t.id === '1')` finds the placeholder entry
8. It replaces the placeholder with the trackOptions
9. But `initAudio(trackOptions)` can't find `url` or `audioBuffer` at the top level
10. So the audio is never loaded → no sound!

THIS is the bug. The fix: In the `addTrack` call from `onAddPhrase`, put the audio data at the top level.

Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The multitrack constructor already handles this correctly by creating flat entries per phrase. But `addTrack` from `onAddPhrase` creates a nested structure.

Looking at how `addTrack` is currently called:
```js
multitrackRef.current.addTrack(trackOptions);
```

Where trackOptions has:
```js
{
    id: track.id,  // '1'
    name: track.name,  // 'Backtrack'

    phrases: [{ url: blob, audioBuffer: buffer, ... }]
}
```

But the `addTrack` method expects the same format as the constructor items — flat, per-phrase entries. So the `addTrack` should actually recreate the flat structure.

Wait, but when `addTrack` replaces the track, it then calls `initWavesurfer` which only handles ONE track. If we were to flatten, we'd need to pass a single-phrase flat entry.

The correct fix would be: instead of passing a track-level object with nested phrases, pass a flat entry (same as constructor items) with the audio data at the top level. Something like:

```js
    id: phrase.id,
    trackId: track.id,
```

Hmm, but there's another issue: after recording, does the multitrack need to show ONLY the new phrase, or all phrases for that track? The constructor puts all phrases as separate entries... no wait, let me re-read. Each track in the store can have multiple phrases, but in the constructor, if a track has phrases, each phrase becomes its own multitrack entry. So track '1' with 3 phrases would create 3 separate multitrack entries (with different IDs), all sharing `trackId: '1'`.

But the `findIndex` in `addTrack` looks for `t.id === track.id`. If track.id is '1', and there are multiple entries with phrases that have different IDs... the findIndex would fail to find a match and fall into the "could not find" warning branch.

Unless... the only entry with `id: '1'` is the placeholder, and after the first recording, it's been replaced. Then the second recording tries `addTrack` with `id: '1'` again, and there's nothing with `id: '1'` left in the tracks array (because the first one was replaced with `id: 'phrase_id_1'`). So `addTrack` would log the warning and do nothing.

OK so this analysis shows that:
1. For the FIRST recording on a track, `addTrack` finds and replaces the placeholder, but audio data is missing (nested)
2. For SUBSEQUENT recordings on the same track, `addTrack` can't find the track at all

For the first case, the fix is to provide audio data at the top level.
For the second case, the track needs to be found by `trackId` instead of by `id`.

Actually wait, let me re-check. The `addTrack` method has:
```js
let index = this.tracks.findIndex((t) => t.id === track.id)
if (index === -1 && track.trackId) {
    index = this.tracks.findIndex((t) => t.trackId === track.trackId)
}
```

It first looks for matching `t.id === track.id`. If not found AND `track.trackId` is set, it looks for `t.trackId === track.trackId`.

In the `onAddPhrase` callback, `trackOptions` has `id: track.id` (which is the track id like '1'). If the track already has a phrase (so the multitrack entry now has `id: 'phrase_id_1'` and `trackId: '1'`), then:
- First check: `t.id === '1'` → no match (the entry now has id 'phrase_id_1')
- Second check: `t.trackId === track.trackId` → but trackOptions doesn't have `trackId` set! It has `id: track.id` but not `trackId: track.id`.

Wait actually, looking at the `trackOptions` from the App.tsx version I already read, `trackOptions` doesn't include `trackId` as a field. Let me check what's actually set...

Looking at the onAddPhrase code:
```js
const trackOptions = {
    id: track.id,
    name: track.name,
    color: track.color,
    isMuted: track.isMuted,
```

No `trackId` field is set. So for a second recording, the `findIndex` would fail.

OK, I think I have a clear understanding now. Let me summarize all the issues and fixes needed.

**ISSUE 1 (Critical): No audio on recorded clips because audio data is nested, not at top level**

The `onAddPhrase` → `addTrack` call creates a trackOptions with audio data nested inside `phrases[0]`. But `initAudio` looks for `track.audioBuffer` and `track.url` at the top level. Neither is found, so the WebAudioPlayer gets no buffer → no audio.

**Fix:** After calling `useStore.getState().addPhrase(trackId, phrase)`, fetch the updated track and pass a properly structured flat entry to `addTrack`. Or restructure the trackOptions to put audio at the top level for the first phrase.

**ISSUE 2 (Medium): Second recording on the same track can't find the track to replace**

After the first recording replaces the placeholder, subsequent `addTrack` calls search for `id === track.id` which no longer matches, and there's no `trackId` in the options to use the fallback.

**Fix:** Include `trackId` in the trackOptions so the fallback search works.

**ISSUE 3 (from original analysis): Worklet anchor frame**
Already fixed.

**ISSUE 4 (from original analysis): UserTime/RealTime conversions**
Already fixed.

**ISSUE 5 (from original analysis): App.tsx effect performance**
Already fixed.

Let me now re-examine the `onAddPhrase` callback with the corrected understanding.

Actually, let me reconsider the approach. Instead of trying to fix the `addTrack` call from `onAddPhrase`, maybe the App.tsx `useEffect` should be responsible for adding tracks to multitrack. The `onAddPhrase` callback should just add the phrase to the store, and the App.tsx effect should detect the new phrase and update the multitrack.

But the App.tsx effect is gated by `syncedTrackIdsRef` which only runs once. For new recordings, the existing track is already in the synced set, so it won't re-run.

I think a cleaner approach is:

1. Remove the `addTrack` call from `onAddPhrase` entirely
2. Make the App.tsx effect properly detect phrase changes and update the multitrack
3. Or, track phrase IDs rather than track IDs in the synced set

Actually, the simplest fix is:
1. In `onAddPhrase`, after adding to store, simply update the App.tsx `syncedTrackIdsRef` to remove the track ID so the useEffect re-runs. But we can't call setState on a ref from inside the callback...

Or better:
1. Add a version counter per track to the state, increment it when a phrase is added
2. Use that as a dependency in the App.tsx effect

Or simplest:
1. Fix the `onAddPhrase` `addTrack` to pass a proper flat structure
2. Add `trackId` to support the fallback search
3. Remove the track from `syncedTrackIdsRef` when adding a new phrase

The cleanest approach for the audio-on-recorded-clips issue:

---

## Analysis: "Recorded clips don't play back (visual only, no audio)"

### Root Cause Identified

After tracing the playback path for recorded clips, I found that `onAddPhrase` → `multitrack.addTrack()` constructs a `trackOptions` object where **audio data is nested inside `phrases[0]`**, but `initAudio()` in `multitrack.ts` looks for `track.audioBuffer` and `track.url` **at the top level**. Neither is found → the `WebAudioPlayer` gets created with no buffer → zero duration → visible in WaveSurfer (peaks data is passed) but no audio.

**Evidence:** In `multitrack.ts:181-207` (`initAudio`):
```js
if (track.audioBuffer && audio instanceof WebAudioPlayer && track.url) {
    audio.loadBuffer(track.url, track.audioBuffer)  // ← never reached
} else if (track.url) {
    audio.src = track.url                            // ← never reached  
}
// Falls through: no audio loaded, Promise resolves immediately
```

The `trackOptions` from `onAddPhrase` (lines 135-155 of `useAudioEngine.ts`) puts `audioBuffer`, `url`, `peaks` inside `phrases: [...]` — invisible to `initAudio`.

### Secondary Issue

`addTrack` in `multitrack.ts` uses `findIndex(t => t.id === track.id)`. After the first recording replaces the placeholder, subsequent recordings on the same track can't find a match (the `trackId` fallback field is missing from the options).

### Plan for Fix

1. **`useAudioEngine.ts` — Flatten the `addTrack` call**: Pass audio data (`url`, `audioBuffer`, `peaks`) at the top level of `trackOptions`, matching the constructor's per-phrase flat format. Use `phrase.id` as `id` and add `trackId` for the fallback search.

2. **`App.tsx` — Fix the `useEffect` guard**: Replace the `syncedTrackIdsRef` (which breaks re-recording on the same track) with a more targeted approach — clear the relevant entry when a new phrase arrives on that track, forcing a rebuild.

3. **`useAudioEngine.ts` — Remove redundant `addTrack` after recording**: Since the App.tsx effect handles track rebuilding correctly (once the audio data format is fixed), remove the direct `addTrack` call from `onAddPhrase` and instead invalidate the synced entry.

**Priority order:**
1. Fix the flat audio data format in `addTrack` options (the core bug)
2. Fix `trackId` fallback for subsequent recordings  
3. Fix the App.tsx effect guard logic
4. Verify with build/tests

Would you like me to proceed with these changes once write access is restored?

---

## Assistant (Plan · Ring 2.6 1T Free · 1662.5s)

_Thinking:_

Let me carefully analyze this second console log to understand the two issues the user reported:

1. **First clip's audio is one beat too early, head seems more than 1 sec**
2. **Second clip was laid in the future and NOT where the playhead was when rec was pressed**

Let me trace through the data:

### First Recording (Track 1)

From the log:
- `12:15:57.552` - `AudioWorklet recording initialized`
- `12:15:57.553` - `callback: seekTo { time: -2, allowNegative: true }`
- `12:15:57.572` - `callback: setIsPlaying true`
- `12:16:02.411` - `stopRecording: called` (4.86 seconds later, which matches duration of 4.77s)
- `12:16:02.449` - `_flush() returning: recordingStartFrame: 11392, anchoredFrame: 102165, rollingOffset: 0, audioDataLength: 210688, sampleRate: 44100`
- `12:16:02.481` - `callback: onAddPhrase { trackId: "1", startPosition: -2.7416780045351477, duration: 4.77750566893424, anchoredFrame: 11392 }`

Let me verify the math:
- `anchoredFrame: 102165` in the worklet's `_flush()` DEBUG message
- But `onAddPhrase` shows `anchoredFrame: 11392`

Wait, let me re-read the worklet code. In `_flush()`:
```js
return [result, returnFrame, rollingOffset];
```
where `returnFrame = this._recordingStartFrame`

And in the main thread `handleAudioWorkletStop`:
```js
const anchorFrame = anchoredFrame ?? 0;
```
where `anchoredFrame` comes from `flushed[1]` which is `this._recordingStartFrame`.

So `anchoredFrame` in the phrase = `_recordingStartFrame` = 11392 (from the DEBUG log: `recordingStartFrame: 11392`).

The worklet DEBUG also shows `anchoredFrame: 102165` which is `this._anchoredFrame` = `Math.floor(punchInUserTime_Real * sampleRate)`.

Now let me check the startPosition calculation:
- `startPosition = (anchorFrame / sampleRate) - secondsPerBar - headLength`
- `startPosition = (11392 / 44100) - 2 - 1`
- `startPosition = 0.258322... - 2 - 1 = -2.741678...`

That matches! `startPosition: -2.7416780045351477`

But wait - the user said they recorded from UserTime 0 with count-in always (1 bar pre-roll). So the expected behavior is:
- User clicks at Bar 1 (UserTime 0)
- Pre-roll: 1 bar count-in, so recording actually starts at RealTime corresponding to UserTime 0
- The clip should appear at UserTime 0 on the timeline

Let me trace the startRecording flow:

The seekTo was called with `time: -2` (which is `punchInUserTime` = 0 - secondsPerBar = -2). That's the pre-roll seek.

Then in startRecording:
```
punchInUserTime = Math.max(0, storeState?.currentTime || 0)
```
Wait, this is when `isCurrentlyPlaying = false` (stopped state), so:
```
punchInUserTime = Math.max(0, storeState?.currentTime || 0)
```

But the store's `currentTime` at that point would be... let me check. The user clicked at UserTime 0 (Bar 1.1). The click handler sets `currentTime` via `seekTo(0)` which sets `storeState.currentTime = 0`.

Actually wait, looking at the click handler:
```
const userTime = realTime - secondsPerBar;
seekTo(userTime);
```
And `seekTo` does `setCurrentTime(finalUserTime)`.

But then in startRecording:
```
punchInUserTime = Math.max(0, storeState?.currentTime || 0);
```

This should give `punchInUserTime = 0`.

Then:
```
recordStartUserTime = effectivePreRollMode !== 'none' ? punchInUserTime - secondsPerBar : punchInUserTime
= 0 - 2 = -2
```

That matches the seekTo of -2.

Now, the `punchInUserTime_Real` calculation (stopped case):
```
const startupDelay = 0.15;
const timeFromPlaybackStartToPunchIn = punchInUserTime - recordStartUserTime = 0 - (-2) = 2
punchInUserTime_Real = audioContext.currentTime + startupDelay + timeFromPlaybackStartToPunchIn
```

The audioContext.currentTime at this point... hmm, the AudioContext was just created/resumed. The `startupDelay` of 0.15 is an approximation.

So `punchInUserTime_Real` is sent to the worklet, and:
```
_anchoredFrame = Math.floor(punchInUserTime_Real * sampleRate)
```

This is meant to be the Real Time frame where the punch-in happens. But the recording doesn't start immediately at `_anchoredFrame` — it starts at `currentFrame` when the START_RECORDING message is processed, which is `this._recordingStartFrame`.

The issue is: `_recordingStartFrame` (when recording actually begins in process()) ≠ `_anchoredFrame` (the calculated punch-in time).

Let me look at the timing more carefully:

From the worklet log:
- `recordingStartFrame: 11392` → `11392 / 44100 = 0.2583s` (Real Time when recording started)
- `anchoredFrame: 102165` → `102165 / 44100 = 2.3166s` (calculated punch-in Real Time)

So the recording actually started at 0.258s Real Time, but the calculated anchor says punch-in should be at 2.317s Real Time. That's a ~2 second difference!

Actually, let me reconsider. The _recordingStartFrame is used in _flush() to determine where the rolling buffer splits:
```js
const recordStartFrame = this._recordingStartFrame;
for (let i = 0; i < this._rollingBuffer.length; i++) {
    const chunk = this._rollingBuffer[i];
    const chunkEndFrame = chunk.frame + chunk.data.length;
    if (chunkEndFrame <= recordStartFrame) {
        allChunks.push(chunk.data);  // pre-roll head
        rollingOffset += chunk.data.length;
    } else if (chunk.frame < recordStartFrame) {
        // partial overlap
        const offset = recordStartFrame - chunk.frame;
        allChunks.push(chunk.data.subarray(0, offset));
        rollingOffset += offset;
        break;
    } else {
        break;
    }
}
// Then append recorded audio data
```

So `_recordingStartFrame` splits the rolling buffer into "pre-recording head" vs "actual recording". If recording starts immediately, `_recordingStartFrame` is very early, and almost nothing from the rolling buffer becomes "head". But the actual audio capture includes everything from that point.

- `result` = all the audio (head + recording)
- `returnFrame` = what gets used as `anchoredFrame`
- `rollingOffset` = how many frames of the result are "head" before the punch-in

Let me reconsider the approach entirely. Maybe instead of trying to calculate `punchInUserTime_Real` from approximations, we should measure it directly from the audio clock.

**Better approach:** When recording starts after pre-roll, the definitive punch-in moment in RealTime is: `playbackStartTime + secondsPerBar`, where `playbackStartTime` is the `audioContext.currentTime` when playback was scheduled.

In `startRecording`:
```js
const recordStartUserTime = punchInUserTime - secondsPerBar  // = -2
```

Wait, `seekTo` does: `const realTime = finalUserTime + secondsPerBar` → `realTime = -2 + 2 = 0`. Then `multitrack.setTime(0)`.

So `multitrack.setTime(0)` is called — playback starts from RealTime 0.

Then after 2 seconds (= secondsPerBar), the playhead reaches UserTime 0. That's the punch-in point.

In RealTime: `punchInRealTime = playbackStartRealTime + secondsPerBar`

But `playbackStartRealTime` is when `setTime(0)` was called. If the audioContext was just created/resumed, `audioContext.currentTime` at that moment is approximately the RealTime origin.

So the correct `punchInUserTime_Real` should be:
```
punchInUserTime_Real = playbackStartRealTime + secondsPerBar
```

Where `playbackStartRealTime` is `audioContext.currentTime` when `setTime(0)` was called.

But in the current code:
```js
punchInUserTime_Real = audioContext.currentTime + startupDelay + timeFromPlaybackStartToPunchIn
```

This uses `audioContext.currentTime` at the moment the START_RECORDING message is sent (which is immediately after `onSetIsPlaying(true)` and `onSetIsRecording(true)`). By this time, `setTime(0)` has been called but `audioContext.currentTime` may have advanced a tiny bit.

The `startupDelay = 0.15` is meant to account for the fact that playback doesn't start instantaneously. But this is an approximation and can be off.

OK let me step back and think about what the cleanest solution is:

**Solution: Record the playback start RealTime and compute anchor from it**

Instead of trying to calculate `punchInUserTime_Real` with delay approximations, we should:
1. Record `audioContext.currentTime` when `seekTo(-2, true)` / `setTime(0)` is called. Call this `playbackStartRealTime`.
2. Compute `punchInRealTime = playbackStartRealTime + secondsPerBar` (for pre-roll mode).
3. Send this exact value to the worklet as `punchInUserTime_Real`.
4. In the worklet, `_anchoredFrame = Math.floor(punchInUserTime_Real * sampleRate)`.
5. Recording starts immediately (to capture rolling buffer).
6. `_recordingStartFrame = currentFrame` when recording starts.
7. `_flush()` returns `[result, _anchoredFrame, rollingOffset]`.

Actually, looking at this from a different direction: what if we eliminate the `startupDelay` approximation? The real question is: when `seekTo(-2, true)` is called, what is the `audioContext.currentTime`?

Looking at the code flow:
1. `onSeekTo(-2, true)` → `realTime = -2 + 2 = 0` → `multitrack.setTime(0)` → `multitrack.seekTo(0/maxDuration)` → `updatePosition(0)` → sets `startAudioTime = audioContext.currentTime` and `startMultitrackTime = 0`

Wait, `seekTo(position)` calls `setTime(position * maxDuration)`. If `position = 0 / maxDuration = 0`, then `setTime(0)`. `setTime` calls `updatePosition(0)`. In `updatePosition`, the sync loop's `startAudioTime` is set to `audioContext.currentTime` on the next frame.

But the `startRecording` continues synchronously:
```js
this.callbacks.onSeekTo(recordStartUserTime, true);
// ... then immediately:
this.callbacks.onSetIsPlaying(true);
this.callbacks.onSetIsRecording(true);
```

And THEN the punchInUserTime_Real is calculated. So `audioContext.currentTime` here is the time right after the synchronous seek and play calls, not the time when playback actually begins.

The issue is fundamental: `audioContext.currentTime` advances continuously, and we're trying to compute the RealTime of a future event (when the playhead reaches the punch-in point). The `startupDelay` is a crude approximation.

**Better approach: Send the punch-in UserTime to the worklet and have the main thread record when playback started, then compute anchor from that.**

Or even simpler: **Don't approximate at all. Let the worklet measure the RealTime directly.**

Here's the clean approach:
1. Send `START_RECORDING` to the worklet with `punchInUserTime` (UserTime, e.g., 0) and `preRollSeconds` (e.g., 2)
2. Worklet starts recording immediately (as it does now)  
3. Worklet computes: `_anchoredFrame = currentFrame + Math.floor(preRollSeconds * sampleRate)`
4. When STOP arrives, `_flush()` returns `_anchoredFrame`

This way, the worklet itself computes the anchor based on when recording ACTUALLY started (measured from `currentFrame`), plus the known pre-roll duration. No approximation from the main thread needed.

But wait, there's still the startup delay issue. The message takes some time to arrive at the worklet. The worklet records from `currentFrame` when it receives the message. The pre-roll should start from the actual playback start, not from when the message arrives.

**For pre-roll mode:**
- Recording starts immediately when the message arrives
- The rolling buffer captures whatever was playing during pre-roll
- `_anchoredFrame` should be `_recordingStartFrame + Math.floor(expectedPreRollFrames)`
- Where `expectedPreRollFrames = secondsPerBar * sampleRate`

This is because recording starts NOW, and the definitive audio will start `secondsPerBar` seconds from now. The rolling buffer will contain the pre-roll audio.

This splits the rolling buffer at the recording start, putting all pre-roll into the head. Then:


```js
public play(startTime?: number) {
    const startAt = startTime || (this.audioContext ? this.audioContext.currentTime + 0.1 : 0);
    this.startSync(startAt)
    indexes.forEach((index) => {
        const audio = this.audios[index] as WebAudioPlayer;
        if (audio && typeof audio.playAt === 'function') {
            audio.playAt(startAt)
        } else if (audio) {
            audio.play()
        }
    })
}
```

After the worklet starts (when `initAudioWorklet` is called):
1. `process()` starts being called. Each call adds 128 frames to the rolling buffer.
2. The rolling buffer trim condition: keep while `currentFrame - rollingBuffer[0].frame <= maxFrames (= 2 * sr = 88200)`
3. The rolling buffer is NEVER trimmed until it exceeds 2 seconds of data, which takes 2 seconds.

So at frame 11392 (0.258s after worklet started):
- Rolling buffer contains frames 0 through ~11392
- `_recordingStartFrame = 11392`

```
    elif chunk.frame < 11392:   // partial overlap
```

The metronome starts at RealTime 0 (when `multitrack.setTime(0)` is called). At RealTime 2 (UserTime 0), the metronome is at beat 1 of Bar 2 (the downbeat that the user is recording to).

For the recorded clip to be in sync at this downbeat, the clip's audio at the recording start point needs to be at position `newTime` when `time = 2`.

If `track.startPosition = S`, then `newTime = 2 - S` at the downbeat.

We want `newTime` to equal the offset into the buffer where the "real" recording starts. If there's a 1-second head:
- Buffer position 0 = start of head

- Time into the clip at the downbeat should be `headLength = 1` second

So: `2 - S = 1 → S = 1` in RealTime

```
= anchorFrame/44100 - 2 - 1
```

`anchorFrame = 2 * 44100 = 88200`

Then `realStartPosition = -1 + 2 + 0 = 1` (RealTime) → `newTime at downbeat = 2 - 1 = 1` → buffer position 1s = start of actual recording after 1s head.

But `punchInUserTime_Real` tries to predict when the audio context will actually start processing audio. Due to `startupDelay`, it adds 0.15s. So it's saying: "the punch-in WILL happen at RealTime 2.15" (accounting for the fact that the `setTime(0)` call has a 100ms scheduling delay).

If the actual audio starts at `audioContext.currentTime + 0.1` (the 0.1 in `play()`), and the timeline was set to RealTime 0, then:
- At `audioContext.currentTime + 0.1`, the playhead is at RealTime 0
- At `audioContext.currentTime + 0.1 + 2`, the playhead is at RealTime 2
- The punch-in happens at `audioContext.currentTime + 0.1 + 2`

In `startRecording`:
```
punchInUserTime_Real = audioContext.currentTime + 0.15 + 2
```

If `audioContext.currentTime` is approximately 0 when `play()` is called, this gives:
```
punchInUserTime_Real ≈ 0 + 0.15 + 2 = 2.15
```

The 0.15 is meant to be the startup delay (0.1s from `play()` + some margin), but it's applied BEFORE the 2-second pre-roll, so it pushes the anchor 0.15s too far.

Actually, the `startupDelay` accounts for the fact that `audioContext.currentTime` might be slightly ahead of the playback start. The formula tries to say: "punch-in RealTime = current audio time + delay until playback start + time from playback start to punch-in." But `audioContext.currentTime` IS already the audio time, and the playback was scheduled to start at `audioContext.currentTime + 0.1`.

**The simplest correct approach:**

Since the recording captures audio starting from whenever the message arrives, and the playhead is already at some position in the timeline:

For "always" pre-roll mode:
1. User clicks Record at UserTime 0
2. Playback was seeked to UserTime -2 (RealTime 0) and starts playing
3. Recording starts immediately (capturing the 2-second count-in as head)
4. After 2 seconds of count-in, the real recording begins

The anchor should simply be: `punchInUserTime + secondsPerBar + (time from context creation to playback start)`

But this requires knowing the exact `time from context creation to playback start`, which varies.

**Alternative approach: Use the worklet to compute the anchor**

Since the worklet has access to `currentFrame` (which is the exact audio clock), we can have it compute the anchor:

1. When `START_RECORDING` is received, record `currentFrame` as `_recordingStartFrame`
2. If pre-roll mode ("always"), the anchor is `_recordingStartFrame + Math.floor(secondsPerBar * sampleRate)`
3. If no pre-roll ("none"), the anchor is `_recordingStartFrame`

This is correct because:
- Recording starts at `_recordingStartFrame`
- In pre-roll mode, the definitive audio starts `secondsPerBar` after recording start
- In no-pre-roll mode, the definitive audio starts immediately

The `secondsPerBar` value needs to be passed to the worklet in the START_RECORDING message.

For the startPosition calculation:
```
startPosition (UserTime) = anchorFrame/sr - sPB - HL
= (recordingStartFrame + sPB * sr)/sr - sPB - HL
= recordingStartFrame/sr + sPB - sPB - HL
= recordingStartFrame/sr - HL
```

Actually, there's an even simpler approach. Let me reconsider the entire architecture.

**The cleanest solution:**

Instead of computing `punchInUserTime_Real` on the main thread, measure the anchor directly in the worklet:

1. Send `START_RECORDING` with:
   - `preRollSeconds` (e.g., 2 for "always" mode, 0 for "none")
   - `startFrame = currentFrame` (always — record immediately)

2. In the worklet:
   ```js
   this._recordingStartFrame = currentFrame;
   this._preRollFrames = Math.floor(preRollSeconds * sampleRate);
   this._anchoredFrame = currentFrame + this._preRollFrames;
   ```

3. `_flush()` returns `[result, this._anchoredFrame, this._preRollFrames]`

4. Main thread's `handleAudioWorkletStop`:
   ```js
   anchorFrame = flushed[1]; // recording start + pre-roll frames
   startPosition = (anchorFrame / sr) - sPB - HL;
   ```

OK I think I now understand the fundamental issue. Let me re-read the design:

**Design intent:**
- `anchoredFrame` = the shared-clock frame of the "definitive audio start" (the punch-in point)
- `headLength` = length of the pre-roll audio that's included before the anchor
- `startPosition` = `anchorFrame/sr - secondsPerBar - headLength` = where the visual clip starts
- The total audio buffer = [head audio of length `headLength`] + [audio from anchor onward]
- Playback: the entire buffer plays from position 0, but the "definitive" audio starts at `headLength` into the buffer

With `headLength = 1`:
- `startPosition = anchorFrame/sr - secondsPerBar - 1`
- At UserTime 0 (anchor), `anchorFrame/sr = secondsPerBar = 2`
- `startPosition = 2 - 2 - 1 = -1`

So the clip should visually span from UserTime -1 to UserTime (duration - 1). The first second of the clip is the "head" (pre-roll), and the rest is the actual recording.

But currently, the buffer contains: [2 seconds of rolling buffer that happened to be before _recordingStartFrame] + [actual recording]. The rolling buffer was continuously capturing during the 2-second pre-roll period, so it has the metronome clicks from Bar 0.

**The real bug:** `_recordingStartFrame` (the actual frame when recording began) and `_anchoredFrame` (the target frame sent from main thread) should be **the same value**, but they're not. `_recordingStartFrame = 11392` (~0.26s after context start) while `_anchoredFrame = 102165` (target at ~2.317s).

The fix should be: instead of `return _recordingStartFrame`, we should return the actual anchor frame that was intentionally set, accounting for the pre-roll. The audio buffer should be structured so that:
1. The head portion = `headLength` seconds of audio BEFORE the anchor
2. The anchor portion = audio FROM the anchor point ONWARD

So the audio buffer should be constructed as:
```
[audio from (anchorFrame - headLength*sr) to anchorFrame] + [audio from anchorFrame onward]
```

And `startPosition = anchorFrame/sr - secondsPerBar - headLength`

Currently, `_flush()` takes all rolling buffer data before `_recordingStartFrame` + all recorded data after `_recordingStartFrame`. If we instead use `_anchoredFrame` as the anchor point, we'd take rolling buffer data before `_anchoredFrame` + recorded data from `_recordingStartFrame` onward. But `_recordingStartFrame` and `_anchoredFrame` are different.

Actually, I think the issue is simpler than I thought. The worklet should record starting from `_anchoredFrame`, not from the current frame when the message is processed. Or alternatively, the `_recordingStartFrame` needs to be adjusted.

Hmm actually this is getting complex. Let me think about what the CORRECT behavior should be:

**Correct flow for recording from stopped with pre-roll:**
1. Seek to RealTime 0 (UserTime -2), start playing
2. Metronome plays count-in for 1 bar (2s)
3. User presses Record at UserTime ~0 (RealTime ~2)
4. System should capture: head (pre-roll audio, e.g., metronome count-in) + user's recording
5. The "anchor" should be at RealTime ~2 (UserTime 0)
6. The visual clip should start at UserTime -2 (RealTime 0) to show the full count-in + recording

**With current `headLength = 1`:**
- `startPosition = anchorFrame/sr - secondsPerBar - headLength`
- At UserTime 0 with secondsPerBar=2: `startPosition = 2 - 2 - 1 = -1`
- This means the clip starts at UserTime -1, which is 1 bar into the count-in (not the full bar)

I think the core issue is that `headLength` defaults to 1 second but the pre-roll is 1 bar (2 seconds in this case). The headLength should match the pre-roll duration for correct visual alignment. Or equivalently, the formula should account for the actual pre-roll duration rather than a configurable headLength.

Let me reconsider the design. The intention is:
- Rolling buffer captures whatever plays during the pre-roll
- This audio is prepended to the recording as the "head"
- `headLength` controls how much of this pre-roll audio is kept
- The "anchor" marks where the definitive recording begins

So the buffer is: `[head audio: headLength seconds before anchor]` + `[definitive audio from anchor onward]`

And `startPosition = anchorFrame/sr - secondsPerBar - headLength`

At UserTime 0: `startPosition = sr_anchor_time - 2 - 1 = 2 - 3 = -1`

The clip spans from UserTime -1 to UserTime -1 + duration. The definitive recording starts 1 second into the clip (at UserTime 0), which is after the 1-second head.

But the pre-roll is 2 seconds (1 bar). So the metronome count-in starts at UserTime -2, but the clip only starts at UserTime -1. The first second of the count-in is MISSING from the visual clip.

This means either:
1. `headLength` should be set to `secondsPerBar` (= 2) to capture the full pre-roll
2. OR the formula needs to account for the full pre-roll differently

The root problem: `_recordingStartFrame` is NOT the anchor point. It's the actual frame when recording started in the audio worklet. But the audio buffer has a rolling buffer head that's the pre-recording audio. The design assumes `_recordingStartFrame - rolling_buffer_start ≈ headLength * sampleRate`, but this isn't true.

I think the correct fix for the worklet is:

Actually, let me step back and think about what the simplest correct implementation would be:

**What we want:**
- Buffer = [headLength seconds of pre-roll] + [actual recording]
- When played: the entire buffer plays. The first `headLength` seconds are the pre-roll. The rest is the actual recording.
- Visual: clip starts at `startPosition = anchorRealTime - secondsPerBar - headLength`
- The anchor point (definitive recording start) is at `headLength` seconds into the buffer

**Implementation in worklet `_flush()`:**
We have the rolling buffer (2 seconds of pre-roll audio) and the recorded data. We want to trim the rolling buffer so only `headLength` seconds of it is included as the head.

Currently the code takes ALL rolling buffer data before `_recordingStartFrame`. Instead, it should take only `headLength * sampleRate` frames of rolling buffer data immediately before `_recordingStartFrame`.

Wait, I realize I'm going about this backwards. Let me re-read the `_flush()` code:

```javascript
_flush() {
    const allChunks = [];
    let rollingOffset = 0;

    if (this._rollingBuffer.length > 0) {
        const recordStartFrame = this._recordingStartFrame;
        for (let i = 0; i < this._rollingBuffer.length; i++) {
            const chunk = this._rollingBuffer[i];
            const chunkEndFrame = chunk.frame + chunk.data.length;
            if (chunkEndFrame <= recordStartFrame) {
                allChunks.push(chunk.data);
                rollingOffset += chunk.data.length;
            } else if (chunk.frame < recordStartFrame) {
                const offset = recordStartFrame - chunk.frame;
                if (offset > 0 && offset < chunk.data.length) {
                    allChunks.push(chunk.data.subarray(0, offset));
                    rollingOffset += offset;
                }
                break;
            } else {
                break;
            }
        }
    }
    // ... append recorded data ...
}
```

This iterates through rolling buffer chunks and takes everything before `recordStartFrame`. The rolling buffer has been trimmed to keep only the last `_rollingBufferDuration * sampleRate` frames. So the amount of data taken as "head" depends on how much time passed between the start of the rolling buffer and the recording start.

If recording starts 0.26s after the rolling buffer started filling:
- Rolling buffer contains 0.26-2.0 seconds of audio before recording start
- The loop takes 0-0.26s of that (everything before `_recordingStartFrame`)
- That's only ~0.26 seconds of head, not 1 second

If recording starts 2.3 seconds after rolling buffer started:
- Rolling buffer has ~2 seconds of data before recording
- The loop takes all ~2 seconds as head
- That's more than the expected 1 second head

So the head amount varies depending on when recording started relative to the rolling buffer. The `headLength` is only used to size the rolling buffer, not to trim the actual head!

The fix should be: trim the rolling buffer data in `_flush()` to only include the last `headLength * sampleRate` frames before `_recordingStartFrame`.

Let me now think about the formula:

If we trim the head to exactly `headLength` seconds:
- Buffer = [headLength seconds of pre-roll] + [actual recording]
- `anchorFrame` = `_recordingStartFrame` (shared clock frame where recording begins)
- `headLengthSeconds = headLength` (the configured value, e.g., 1.0)
- The anchor point in RealTime = `_recordingStartFrame / sampleRate`
- `startPosition = anchorFrame/sr - secondsPerBar - headLength`
  = `_recordingStartFrame/sr - secondsPerBar - headLength`

With the first recording:
- `_recordingStartFrame = 11392`
- `startPosition = 11392/44100 - 2 - 1 = 0.258 - 3 = -2.742`

But the recording started at UserTime 0! So the clip should start at approximately UserTime -2 (the start of the pre-roll). The `headLength` of 1 second only captures the last second of the pre-roll, and the formula subtracts the full `secondsPerBar` to position the clip.

I think the fundamental issue is the relationship between `punchInUserTime_Real`, `_recordingStartFrame`, and the rolling buffer. Let me reconsider:

When `startRecording` is called:
1. It calculates `punchInUserTime_Real = audioContext.currentTime + startupDelay + timeFromPlaybackToPunchIn`
2. It immediately sends `START_RECORDING` to the worklet with this value
3. The worklet handles the message in the NEXT `process()` call
4. The worklet sets `_recordingStartFrame = currentFrame` at that time

The gap between step 2 and step 4 is typically 1-3 audio buffer periods (~5-15ms). During this gap, the rolling buffer fills with more audio.

So when `_flush()` runs:
- `_recordingStartFrame` is 1-3 buffer periods after `_anchoredFrame`
- Rolling buffer data between `_recordingStartFrame` and `_anchoredFrame` is lost (not in the head, not in the recording)

To fix this properly:
- `_flush()` should use `_anchoredFrame` as the division point between head and recording
- Rolling buffer data from `_anchoredFrame` onward until `_recordingStartFrame` should be included in the "recording" portion (or at least not discarded)

Actually, I think the simplest fix is:
1. In `_flush()`, use `_anchoredFrame` as the split point (not `_recordingStartFrame`)
2. The "head" = rolling buffer data before `_anchoredFrame`
3. The "recording" = rolling buffer data from `_anchoredFrame` to `_recordingStartFrame` + data recorded after `_recordingStartFrame`

This way, the split happens exactly at the intended anchor point, and no audio is lost.

Hmm, I'm going in circles. Let me just focus on the practical issues and clear fixes:

**Practical Issue #1: rollingOffset = 0 for Recording 2**

The rolling buffer had audio from ~50.38 to ~52.38 (2 seconds). `_recordingStartFrame` = 52.38 * 44100 = 2310272. Rolling buffer covers frames from ~50.38*44100 = 2221380 to 52.38*44100 = 2310272. All data in the rolling buffer has frames < `_recordingStartFrame`, so it should ALL be included as head.

But the log shows `rollingOffset: 0`. That means the `_flush()` loop found nothing to include!

Let me re-read `_flush()`:
```javascript
if (this._rollingBuffer.length > 0) {
    const recordStartFrame = this._recordingStartFrame;
    for (let i = 0; i < this._rollingBuffer.length; i++) {
        const chunk = this._rollingBuffer[i];
        const chunkEndFrame = chunk.frame + chunk.data.length;
        if (chunkEndFrame <= recordStartFrame) {
            allChunks.push(chunk.data);
            rollingOffset += chunk.data.length;
        } else if (chunk.frame < recordStartFrame) {
            const offset = recordStartFrame - chunk.frame;
            if (offset > 0 && offset < chunk.data.length) {
                allChunks.push(chunk.data.subarray(0, offset));
                rollingOffset += offset;
            }
            break;
        } else {
            break;
        }
    }
}
```

The rolling buffer trims data where `currentFrame - rollingBuffer[0].frame > maxFrames`. `maxFrames = 2.0 * sampleRate = 88200`. So it keeps only the last 2 seconds.

If recording started at frame 2310272, the rolling buffer would contain data from frame (2310272 - 88200) = 2222072 onward. So chunks should have frames starting from ~2222072.

But wait, look at the trimming logic:
```javascript
while (this._rollingBuffer.length > 0) {
    if (currentFrame - this._rollingBuffer[0].frame > maxFrames) {
        this._rollingBuffer.shift();
    } else {
        break;
    }
}
```

This trims based on `currentFrame - chunk.frame > maxFrames`. But `currentFrame` here is the frame of the CURRENT `process()` call (when trimming happens). So the buffer keeps data from the most recent `maxFrames` frames relative to NOW.

For the pre-roll case:
- Playback starts at RealTime ~50.24 (when user clicks Record)
- Recording starts at RealTime ~52.39 (after ~2 seconds of pre-roll)
- During those 2 seconds, the rolling buffer accumulates data
- Each process() call trims old data, keeping the last 2 seconds

By the time recording stops, the rolling buffer has data from the last 2 seconds. Since recording lasted ~4.5 seconds, the rolling buffer at stop time has data from (stopFrame - 2s) to stopFrame. Since recording started at frame 2310272 and the buffer keeps the last 2 seconds of data, the buffer has data starting from frame (lastProcessFrame - 88200).

BUT — the key question: is `_recordingStartFrame` LESS than the earliest frame in the rolling buffer? If `_recordingStartFrame = 2310272` and the rolling buffer has frames from ~2316766 (currentFrame at stop - 88200), then YES — the entire rolling buffer is AFTER `_recordingStartFrame`!

In `_flush()`, the loop checks `chunk.frame < recordStartFrame`. If all chunks have `chunk.frame > recordStartFrame`, the loop never enters the `if` branches and breaks immediately. So `rollingOffset = 0` — **NO head audio is included!**

**THIS IS THE BUG!** After recording for a few seconds, the rolling buffer (which always keeps only the LAST 2 seconds) no longer contains any data from before the recording start frame. The early pre-roll audio was already trimmed by the rolling buffer's cleanup.

**Fix:** The rolling buffer must capture and RETAIN enough data from before the recording start. We need to "freeze" the rolling buffer at the moment recording starts, saving the appropriate amount of head audio.

Here's my recommended fix:

1. When recording starts, snapshot the rolling buffer up to `_recordingStartFrame` (or `_anchoredFrame`)
2. This snapshot becomes the head audio
3. Trim the snapshot to exactly `headLength` seconds
4. In `_flush()`, use the snapshot + recorded data

OR simpler: increase the rolling buffer size to always be at least `headLength + recordingDuration` seconds. But we don't know the recording duration in advance.

OR: When recording starts, copy the pre-roll portion of the rolling buffer into a separate `_preRollBuffer`, then `_flush()` uses `_preRollBuffer` + `_audioData`.
