```
1: import { useEffect, useRef, useState, useCallback, useMemo } from 'react';
2: import WaveSurfer from 'wavesurfer.js';
3: import MultiTrack, { type TrackOptions } from '../lib/multitrack/multitrack';
4: import WebAudioPlayer from '../lib/multitrack/webaudio';
5: import RegionsPlugin from 'wavesurfer.js/dist/plugins/regions.esm.js';
6: import { useStore } from '../store/useStore';
7: import { audioBufferToWav } from '../audio/processing/audioBufferToWav';
8: import { calculatePeaksAsync } from '../audio/processing/audioUtils';
9: import { RecordingEngine, RecordingConfig, RecordingCallbacks } from '../audio/recording/RecordingEngine';
10: import { MetronomeEngine } from '../audio/metronome/MetronomeEngine';
11: import { perfLogger } from '../utils/PerformanceLogger';
12: 
13: // Override WaveSurfer.prototype.load to catch unhandled promise rejections globally
14: const originalLoad = WaveSurfer.prototype.load;
15: WaveSurfer.prototype.load = function(this: WaveSurfer, ...args: unknown[]) {
16:   const promise = (originalLoad as (...args: unknown[]) => Promise<void>).apply(this, args);
17:   if (promise && typeof promise.catch === 'function') {
18:     promise.catch((e: Error) => {
19:       if (e && e.name === 'AbortError') return;
20:       console.warn("WaveSurfer load error:", e);
21:     });
22:   }
23:   return promise;
24: };
25: 
26: // Override WaveSurfer.prototype.play to catch unhandled promise rejections globally
27: const originalPlay = WaveSurfer.prototype.play;
28: WaveSurfer.prototype.play = function(this: WaveSurfer, ...args: unknown[]) {
29:   const promise = (originalPlay as (...args: unknown[]) => Promise<void>).apply(this, args);
30:   if (promise && typeof promise.catch === 'function') {
31:     promise.catch((e: Error) => {
32:       if (e && e.name === 'AbortError') return;
33:       if (e && e.message && e.message.includes('aborted by the user agent')) return;
34:       console.warn("WaveSurfer play error:", e);
35:     });
36:   }
37:   return promise;
38: };
39: 
40: const SILENT_WAV = 'data:audio/wav;base64,UklGRigAAABXQVZFZm10IBIAAAABAAEARKwAAIhYAQACABAAAABkYXRhAgAAAAEA';
41: 
42: export function useAudioEngine() {
43:   const rawRecordingMode = useStore(state => state.rawRecordingMode);
44:   const containerRef = useRef<HTMLDivElement>(null);
45:   const multitrackRef = useRef<MultiTrack | null>(null);
46:   const audioContextRef = useRef<AudioContext | null>(null);
47:   const metronomeBufferRef = useRef<{bpm: number, timeSignature: [number, number], buffer: AudioBuffer} | null>(null);
48:   const isPreRollingRef = useRef(false);
49:   const [multitrack, setMultitrack] = useState<MultiTrack | null>(null);
50:   
51:   // Engine refs for modular audio architecture
52:   const metronomeEngineRef = useRef<MetronomeEngine | null>(null);
53:   const recordingEngineRef = useRef<RecordingEngine | null>(null);
54:   const wasPlayingRef = useRef(false);
55:   // Dummy state to force a retry when AudioContext becomes available
56:   // AudioContext initialization & retry mechanism
57: const { 
58:   tracks, 
59:   zoom, 
60:   isPlaying,
61:   isRecording,
62:   selectedTrackId,
63:   selectedPhraseId,
64:   envelopeLocked,
65:   waveformQuality,
66:   bpm,
67:   timeSignature,
68:   preRollMode,
69:   currentTime,
70:   globalLatencyMs,
71:   extraLatencyMs,
72:   duration,
73:   setIsPlaying,
74:   setIsRecording,
75:   setCurrentTime,
76:   setDuration,
77:   moveLocked,
78:   isReady,
79:   setIsReady,
80:   trackHeight,
81:   metronomeHeight
82: } = useStore();
83: 
84:   const beatsPerSecond = (bpm || 120) / 60;
85:   const secondsPerBeat = 1 / beatsPerSecond;
86:   const secondsPerBar = secondsPerBeat * (timeSignature?.[0] || 4);
87: 
88:    
89:    // Initialize and update audio engines
90:   useEffect(() => {
91:     // Initialize MetronomeEngine when audioContext is available
92:     if (audioContextRef.current && !metronomeEngineRef.current) {
93:       metronomeEngineRef.current = new MetronomeEngine(audioContextRef.current);
94:     }
95: 
96:     // Initialize RecordingEngine (AudioWorklet is mandatory)
97:     if (!recordingEngineRef.current) {
98:       const config: RecordingConfig = {
99:         rawRecordingMode,
100:         globalLatencyMs: globalLatencyMs || 0,
101:         extraLatencyMs: extraLatencyMs || 0,
102:         bpm: bpm || 120,
103:         timeSignature: timeSignature || [4, 4],
104:         preRollMode,
105:         isPlaying,
106:         currentTime,
107:         headLength: useStore.getState().headLength,
108:         audioContextRef,
109:       };
110: 
111:       const callbacks: RecordingCallbacks = {
112:         onSetIsPlaying: (playing) => {
113:           console.log('callback: setIsPlaying', playing);
114:           setIsPlaying(playing);
115:         },
116:         onSetIsRecording: (recording) => {
117:           console.log('callback: setIsRecording', recording);
118:           setIsRecording(recording);
119:         },
120:         onAddPhrase: (trackId, phrase) => {
121:           console.log('callback: onAddPhrase', { trackId, startPosition: phrase.startPosition, duration: phrase.duration, anchoredFrame: phrase.anchoredFrame });
122:           useStore.getState().addPhrase(trackId, phrase);
123:           // DIRECT: Also update multitrack immediately so it gets the anchoredFrame
124:           const track = useStore.getState().tracks.find(t => t.id === trackId);
125:           if (track && multitrackRef.current && phrase.anchoredFrame !== undefined) {
126:             console.log('useAudioEngine: DIRECT update multitrack track', trackId, 'with anchoredFrame=', phrase.anchoredFrame);
127:             // Update track.anchoredFrame
128:             track.anchoredFrame = phrase.anchoredFrame;
129:             // FIXED: Convert UserTime → RealTime for multitrack (same as initial construction)
130:             // multitrack stores startPosition in RealTime (AudioContext clock)
131:             const isMetronome = trackId === 'metronome';
132:             const trackOffset = track.offset || 0;
133:             console.log('useAudioEngine: addTrack startPosition conversion — UserTime:', phrase.startPosition, '→ RealTime:', isMetronome ? phrase.startPosition : phrase.startPosition + secondsPerBar + trackOffset, '(sPB:', secondsPerBar, 'offset:', trackOffset, ')');
134:             // Convert Track to TrackOptions (multitrack expects this)
135:             const trackOptions = {
136:               id: track.id,
137:               name: track.name,
138:               color: track.color,
139:               isMuted: track.isMuted,
140:               isSolo: track.isSolo,
141:               volume: track.volume,
142:               pan: track.pan,
143:               offset: track.offset,
144:               anchoredFrame: track.anchoredFrame,
145:               phrases: track.phrases.map(p => ({
146:                 url: p.url,
147:                 audioBuffer: p.audioBuffer,
148:                 peaks: p.peaks,
149:                 startPosition: isMetronome ? p.startPosition : p.startPosition + secondsPerBar + trackOffset,
150:                 duration: p.duration,
151:                 headLength: p.headLength,
152:                 anchoredFrame: p.anchoredFrame,
153:                 originalAnchoredFrame: p.originalAnchoredFrame,
154:               })),
155:             } as any;
156:             multitrackRef.current.addTrack(trackOptions);
157:           }
158:         },
159:         onSeekTo: (time, allowNegative) => {
160:           console.log('callback: seekTo', { time, allowNegative });
161:           seekTo(time, allowNegative);
162:         },
163:         getStoreState: () => useStore.getState(),
164:       };
165: 
166:       recordingEngineRef.current = new RecordingEngine(config, callbacks);
167:     }
168: 
169:     // Update RecordingEngine config (excluding isPlaying - handled separately)
170:     if (recordingEngineRef.current) {
171:       recordingEngineRef.current.updateConfig({
172:         rawRecordingMode,
173:         globalLatencyMs: globalLatencyMs || 0,
174:         extraLatencyMs: extraLatencyMs || 0,
175:         bpm: bpm || 120,
176:         timeSignature: timeSignature || [4, 4],
177:         preRollMode,
178:         currentTime,
179:         headLength: useStore.getState().headLength,
180:         isPlaying,
181:       });
182:     }
183: 
184:     // Update MetronomeEngine config
185:     if (metronomeEngineRef.current) {
186:       metronomeEngineRef.current.updateConfig({
187:         bpm: bpm || 120,
188:         timeSignature: timeSignature || [4, 4],
189:         isPlaying,
190:       });
191:     }
192: 
193:     return () => {
194:       if (recordingEngineRef.current) {
195:         recordingEngineRef.current.cleanup();
196:       }
197:       if (metronomeEngineRef.current) {
198:         metronomeEngineRef.current.cleanup();
199:       }
200:     };
201:   }, [rawRecordingMode, globalLatencyMs, extraLatencyMs, bpm, timeSignature, preRollMode]);
202: 
203:   // Separate effect to update isPlaying without destroying audioWorkletNode
204:   useEffect(() => {
205:     if (recordingEngineRef.current) {
206:       recordingEngineRef.current.updateConfig({ isPlaying });
207:     }
208:   }, [isPlaying]);
209: 
210:   const lastZoomRef = useRef<number>(zoom);
211:   const lastVolumesRef = useRef<Map<number, number>>(new Map());
212:   const lastTimeUpdateRef = useRef<number>(0);
213: 
214:   const adjustLayout = useCallback(() => {
215:     if (!containerRef.current || !multitrackRef.current) return;
216:     
217:     containerRef.current.classList.add('multitrack-container');
218:     
219:     const expandedHeight = Math.floor(trackHeight * 1.5);
220:     const normalHeight = trackHeight;
221:     const currentMetronomeHeight = metronomeHeight;
222:     
223:     // Use tracks state directly
224:     const currentTracks = tracks || [];
225:     
226:     let totalHeight = 0;
227:     currentTracks.forEach(t => {
228:       const isMetronome = t.id === 'metronome';
229:       const isExpanded = t.id === selectedTrackId && !envelopeLocked && !isMetronome;
230:       totalHeight += isMetronome ? currentMetronomeHeight : (isExpanded ? expandedHeight : normalHeight);
231:     });
232: 
233:     let css = `
234:       :root {
235:         --expanded-track-h: ${expandedHeight}px;
236:         --normal-track-h: ${normalHeight}px;
237:       }
238:       .multitrack-container > div > div {
239:         height: ${totalHeight}px !important;
240:         min-height: ${totalHeight}px !important;
241:         max-height: ${totalHeight}px !important;
242:         overflow: hidden !important;
243:       }
244:     `;
245: 
246:     let previousTrackId: string | null = null;
247:     const multitrackItems = (multitrackRef.current as { tracks: TrackOptions[] }).tracks || [];
248:     
249:     // Check if DOM elements are rendered
250:     const trackElements = document.querySelectorAll('.multitrack-container > div > div > div');
251:     
252:     // If multitrack hasn't populated tracks yet, or DOM elements aren't rendered, retry shortly
253:     if (multitrackItems.length === 0 || trackElements.length === 0) {
254:       setTimeout(adjustLayout, 100);
255:       return;
256:     }
257: 
258:     for (let i = 0; i < multitrackItems.length; i++) {
259:       const item = multitrackItems[i];
260:       const childIndex = i + 2; 
261:       
262:       const isNewTrack = item.trackId !== previousTrackId;
263:       const track = currentTracks.find(t => t.id === item.trackId);
264:       const isMetronome = track?.id === 'metronome';
265:       const isExpanded = track?.id === selectedTrackId && !envelopeLocked && !isMetronome;
266:       const h = isMetronome ? currentMetronomeHeight : (isExpanded ? expandedHeight : normalHeight);
267: 
268:       css += `
269:         .multitrack-container > div > div > div:nth-child(${childIndex}) {
270:           z-index: ${isMetronome ? 1000 : 10 + i} !important;
271:           height: ${h}px !important;
272:           opacity: 0.8 !important;
273:           mix-blend-mode: screen !important;
274:           ${!isNewTrack ? `margin-top: -${h}px !important;` : `margin-top: 0px !important;`}
275:         }
276:       `;
277:       
278:       if (isNewTrack && previousTrackId !== null) {
279:         css += `
280:           .multitrack-container > div > div > div:nth-child(${childIndex})::before {
281:             content: '';
282:             position: absolute;
283:             top: 0;
284:             left: 0;
285:             right: 0;
286:             height: 2px;
287:             background-color: #4A5568;
288:             z-index: 50;
289:             pointer-events: none;
290:           }
291:         `;
292:       }
293:       
294:       previousTrackId = item.trackId;
295:     }
296: 
297:     let styleEl = document.getElementById('multitrack-custom-layout');
298:     if (!styleEl) {
299:       styleEl = document.createElement('style');
300:       styleEl.id = 'multitrack-custom-layout';
301:       document.head.appendChild(styleEl);
302:     }
303:     styleEl.textContent = css;
304: 
305:     // Layout is now applied, we can show the UI
306:     setIsReady(true);
307:     if (containerRef.current) {
308:       containerRef.current.style.opacity = '1';
309:     }
310:   }, [selectedTrackId, envelopeLocked, tracks, setIsReady, trackHeight, metronomeHeight]);
311: 
312:   const trackStructureHash = useMemo(() => {
313:     // Only hash the structural elements that require a full engine rebuild
314:     return (tracks || []).map(t => 
315:       `${t.id}:${(t.phrases || []).map(p => `${p.id}:${p.url}`).join('|')}`
316:     ).join(',') + `|bpm:${bpm}|ts:${timeSignature?.[0]}/${timeSignature?.[1]}`;
317:   }, [tracks, bpm, timeSignature]);
318: 
319: // Initialize Multitrack
320:   useEffect(() => {
321:     if (!containerRef.current) return;
322: 
323:     perfLogger.log(1);
324:     setIsReady(false);
325:     if (containerRef.current) {
326:       containerRef.current.style.opacity = '0';
327:       containerRef.current.style.transition = 'opacity 0.3s ease-in-out';
328:     }
329: 
330:     let disposed = false;
331: 
332:     (async () => {
333:       try {
334:         if (!audioContextRef.current || audioContextRef.current.state === 'closed') {
335:           // Create AudioContext if not exists or closed
336:           audioContextRef.current = new (window.AudioContext || (window as (typeof window & { webkitAudioContext: typeof AudioContext })).webkitAudioContext)();
337:         }
338: 
339:         // Resume if suspended - fire-and-forget since this requires a user gesture
340:         // and will resolve on first click/keypress. Don't block init on this.
341:         if (audioContextRef.current?.state === 'suspended') {
342:           audioContextRef.current.resume().catch(() => {/* expected on page load */});
343:         }
344: 
345:         // Ensure AudioContext is not null
346:         if (!audioContextRef.current) {
347:           throw new Error('Failed to create AudioContext');
348:         }
349: 
350:         // Update sample rate in store
351:         useStore.getState().setSampleRate(audioContextRef.current.sampleRate);
352: 
353:         const multitrackItems: (TrackOptions & { trackId: string })[] = [];
354: 
355:         const pixelRatio = waveformQuality === 'low' ? 1 : waveformQuality === 'medium' ? Math.max(1, window.devicePixelRatio / 2) : window.devicePixelRatio;
356:         const sampleRate = waveformQuality === 'low' ? 8000 : waveformQuality === 'medium' ? 16000 : 44100;
357: 
358:         const beatsPerSecond = (bpm || 120) / 60;
359:         const secondsPerBeat = 1 / beatsPerSecond;
360:         const secondsPerBar = secondsPerBeat * (timeSignature?.[0] || 4);
361: 
362:         (tracks || []).forEach(t => {
363:           const isMetronome = t.id === 'metronome';
364:           if (t.phrases.length === 0) {
365:             multitrackItems.push({
366:               id: t.id,
367:               trackId: t.id,
368:               url: SILENT_WAV,
369:               startPosition: 0,
370:               volume: 0,
371:                 options: {
372:                   waveColor: t.color,
373:                   progressColor: t.color,
374:                   height: isMetronome ? metronomeHeight : (t.id === selectedTrackId && !envelopeLocked ? Math.floor(trackHeight * 1.5) : trackHeight),
375:                   pixelRatio,
376:                   sampleRate,
377:                 } as unknown as TrackOptions['options']
378:             });
379:           } else {
380:             t.phrases.forEach(p => {
381:               const realStartPosition = isMetronome ? p.startPosition : p.startPosition + secondsPerBar + (t.offset || 0);
382: 
383:               multitrackItems.push({
384:                 id: p.id,
385:                 trackId: t.id,
386:                 url: p.url,
387:                 audioBuffer: p.audioBuffer,
388:                 peaks: p.peaks,
389:                 startPosition: realStartPosition,
390:                 startCue: p.startCue,
391:                 endCue: p.endCue,
392:                 headLength: p.headLength,
393:                 anchoredFrame: p.anchoredFrame,
394:                 originalAnchoredFrame: p.originalAnchoredFrame,
395:                 volume: t.isMuted ? 0 : t.volume,
396:                 draggable: true,
397:                 options: {
398:                   waveColor: t.color,
399:                   progressColor: t.color,
400:                   height: isMetronome ? metronomeHeight : (t.id === selectedTrackId && !envelopeLocked ? Math.floor(trackHeight * 1.5) : trackHeight),
401:                   pixelRatio,
402:                   sampleRate,
403:                 } as unknown as TrackOptions['options']
404:               });
405:             });
406:           }
407:         });
408: 
409:         if (disposed) return;
410: 
411:         const multitrack = MultiTrack.create(multitrackItems, {
412:           container: containerRef.current,
413:           minPxPerSec: zoom,
414:           rightButtonDrag: false,
415:           cursorWidth: 2,
416:           cursorColor: '#D72F21',
417:           trackBackground: '#2D3748',
418:           audioContext: audioContextRef.current!,
419:           moveLocked: moveLocked,
420:           timelineOptions: {
421:             formatTimeCallback: (seconds: number) => {
422:               const userTime = seconds - secondsPerBar;
423:               const absTime = Math.abs(userTime);
424:               const mins = Math.floor(absTime / 60);
425:               const secs = Math.floor(absTime % 60);
426:               const sign = userTime < 0 ? '-' : '';
427:               return `${sign}${mins}:${secs.toString().padStart(2, '0')}`;
428:             }
429:           },
430:           preRollDuration: secondsPerBar
431:         });
432: 
433:         multitrackRef.current = multitrack;
434:         setMultitrack(multitrack);
435:         useStore.getState().setMultitrackTimeGetter(() => {
436:           const realTime = multitrack.getCurrentTime();
437:           return realTime - secondsPerBar;
438:         });
439: 
440:         multitrack.on('canplay', () => {
441:           const storeState = useStore.getState();
442:           const realTime = storeState.currentTime + secondsPerBar;
443: 
444:           if (typeof multitrack.setTime === 'function') {
445:             multitrack.setTime(realTime);
446:           }
447: 
448:           adjustLayout();
449: 
450:           let maxDuration = 0;
451:           const wssList = multitrack.wavesurfers || [];
452:           wssList.forEach((ws: WaveSurfer) => {
453:             try {
454:               const wsTrackId = (ws as any).options?.trackId || (ws as any).options?.id;
455:               if (wsTrackId === 'metronome' || wsTrackId === 'placeholder') return;
456: 
457:               const matchingItems = multitrackItems.filter(item => item.trackId === wsTrackId);
458:               if (matchingItems.length === 0) return;
459: 
460:               const d = ws.getDuration();
461:               const item = matchingItems[0];
462:               const realStartPos = item.startPosition || 0;
463:               const userStartPos = realStartPos - secondsPerBar;
464:               if (d + userStartPos > maxDuration) maxDuration = d + userStartPos;
465:             } catch {
466:               // ignore
467:             }
468:           });
469:           setDuration(maxDuration);
470:         });
471:       } catch (err) {
472:         console.error('Multitrack initialization failed:', err);
473:       }
474:     })();
475: 
476:     return () => {
477:       disposed = true;
478:       if (multitrackRef.current) {
479:         multitrackRef.current.destroy();
480:         multitrackRef.current = null;
481:       }
482:     };
483:     // eslint-disable-next-line react-hooks/exhaustive-deps
484:   }, [trackStructureHash, waveformQuality, bpm, setDuration, timeSignature?.[0], timeSignature?.[1], secondsPerBar]);
485: 
486:   useEffect(() => {
487:     if (!multitrack) return;
488: 
489:     // Remove redundant currentTime listener as we use pollTime for more control
490:     
491:     // Listen for clicks on individual wavesurfers to select phrases
492:     multitrack.on('click', (event: { id: string }) => {
493:       if (event?.id) {
494:         const state = useStore.getState();
495:         state.setSelectedPhraseId(event.id);
496:         
497:         // Also select the track
498:         for (const track of state.tracks) {
499:           if (track.phrases.some(p => p.id === event.id)) {
500:             state.setSelectedTrackId(track.id);
501:             break;
502:           }
503:         }
504:       }
505:     });
506: 
507:     // Listen for drag events to update the store
508:     multitrack.on('start-position-change', (event: { id: string; startPosition: number }) => {
509:       // wavesurfer-multitrack passes an object with { id, startPosition }
510:       // startPosition from multitrack is in RealTime (AudioContext clock)
511:       // We need to convert to UserTime (timeline position) for the store
512:       const id = event?.id;
513:       const realStartPosition = event?.startPosition;
514:       
515:       if (!id || realStartPosition === undefined) return;
516:       
517:       const state = useStore.getState();
518:       for (const track of state.tracks) {
519:         if (track.phrases.some(p => p.id === id)) {
520:           const phrase = track.phrases.find(p => p.id === id);
521:           if (!phrase) break;
522: 
523:           // FIXED: Convert RealTime → UserTime before storing
524:           // RealTime = UserTime + secondsPerBar + trackOffset
525:           // So: UserTime = RealTime - secondsPerBar - trackOffset
526:           const isMetronome = track.id === 'metronome';
527:           const trackOffset = track.offset || 0;
528:           const userStartPosition = isMetronome ? realStartPosition : realStartPosition - secondsPerBar - trackOffset;
529: 
530:           // Only update if the position actually changed to avoid infinite loops
531:           if (Math.abs(phrase.startPosition - userStartPosition) > 0.01) {
532:             console.log('start-position-change: RealTime=', realStartPosition, '→ UserTime=', userStartPosition, '(sPB=', secondsPerBar, 'offset=', trackOffset, ')');
533:             state.updatePhrasePosition(track.id, id, userStartPosition);
534:           }
535:           break;
536:         }
537:       }
538:     });
539:   }, [multitrack, secondsPerBar, setCurrentTime]);
540: 
541:   // Safe polling mechanism for currentTime that doesn't trigger excessive re-renders
542:   useEffect(() => {
543:     let isMounted = true;
544:     let animationFrameId: number;
545:     
546:     const pollTime = () => {
547:       if (!isMounted) return;
548:       
549:       if (multitrackRef.current && typeof multitrackRef.current.getCurrentTime === 'function') {
550:         try {
551:           const state = useStore.getState();
552:           const realTime = multitrackRef.current.getCurrentTime();
553:           const userTime = realTime - secondsPerBar;
554:           
555:           // Requirement 1 & 5: Clamp userTime to 0 for display and store, unless recording or pre-rolling
556:           const isPreRolling = state.isRecording || isPreRollingRef.current;
557:           const clampedUserTime = isPreRolling ? userTime : Math.max(0, userTime);
558:           
559:           // If we were pre-rolling and just passed Bar 1.1 (0s), hide the PRE bar
560:           if (isPreRollingRef.current && userTime >= 0 && !state.isRecording) {
561:             isPreRollingRef.current = false;
562:             if (multitrackRef.current) {
563:               multitrackRef.current.setRecordingMode(false);
564:             }
565:           }
566:           
567:           // Increase resolution to 100ms for smoother UI while reducing re-renders
568:           const stateTime = state.currentTime;
569:           const now = Date.now();
570:           if (typeof clampedUserTime === 'number' && !isNaN(clampedUserTime) && (Math.abs(clampedUserTime - stateTime) > 0.1 || (state.isPlaying && now - lastTimeUpdateRef.current > 100))) {
571:             state.setCurrentTime(clampedUserTime);
572:             lastTimeUpdateRef.current = now;
573:           }
574:           
575:           // Stop playback if we reached the end of the project duration
576:           const effectiveDuration = Math.max(60, state.duration);
577:           if (state.isPlaying && userTime >= effectiveDuration && effectiveDuration > 0 && !state.isRecording) {
578:             state.setIsPlaying(false);
579:             if (multitrackRef.current && typeof multitrackRef.current.pause === 'function') {
580:               multitrackRef.current.pause();
581:             }
582:           }
583:           
584:           // Apply envelope automation
585:           const currentTracks = state.tracks || [];
586:           const anySolo = currentTracks.some(t => t.isSolo);
587:           
588:           let itemIndex = 0;
589:           
590:           currentTracks.forEach(track => {
591:             let trackBaseVolume = track.volume;
592:             if (track.isMuted) trackBaseVolume = 0;
593:             if (anySolo && !track.isSolo) trackBaseVolume = 0;
594:             
595:             // Apply envelope
596:             let envVal = 1;
597:             if (track.envelope && track.envelope.length > 0) {
598:               const nodes = track.envelope;
599:               if (userTime <= nodes[0].time) {
600:                 envVal = nodes[0].value;
601:               } else if (userTime >= nodes[nodes.length - 1].time) {
602:                 envVal = nodes[nodes.length - 1].value;
603:               } else {
604:                 // Interpolate
605:                 for (let i = 0; i < nodes.length - 1; i++) {
606:                   if (userTime >= nodes[i].time && userTime <= nodes[i+1].time) {
607:                     const t0 = nodes[i].time;
608:                     const v0 = nodes[i].value;
609:                     const t1 = nodes[i+1].time;
610:                     const v1 = nodes[i+1].value;
611:                     const ratio = (userTime - t0) / (t1 - t0);
612:                     envVal = v0 + (v1 - v0) * ratio;
613:                     break;
614:                   }
615:                 }
616:               }
617:             }
618:             
619:             const targetVolume = trackBaseVolume * envVal;
620: 
621:             const wsList = multitrackRef.current.wavesurfers || [];
622:             const count = track.phrases.length === 0 ? 1 : track.phrases.length;
623:             
624:             for (let j = 0; j < count; j++) {
625:               const ws = wsList[itemIndex];
626:               if (ws && typeof ws.setVolume === 'function') {
627:                 const lastVol = lastVolumesRef.current.get(itemIndex);
628:                 if (lastVol === undefined || Math.abs(lastVol - targetVolume) > 0.001) {
629:                   ws.setVolume(targetVolume);
630:                   lastVolumesRef.current.set(itemIndex, targetVolume);
631:                 }
632:               }
633:               itemIndex++;
634:             }
635:           });
636:           
637:           // Handle sync loop
638:           const syncLoop = useStore.getState().syncLoop;
639:           if (syncLoop && userTime >= syncLoop.end) {
640:             if (typeof multitrackRef.current.setTime === 'function') {
641:               multitrackRef.current.setTime(syncLoop.start + secondsPerBar);
642:             }
643:           }
644:         } catch {
645:           // Ignore errors during polling
646:         }
647:       }
648:       animationFrameId = requestAnimationFrame(pollTime);
649:     };
650:     
651:     animationFrameId = requestAnimationFrame(pollTime);
652: 
653:     return () => {
654:       isMounted = false;
655:       cancelAnimationFrame(animationFrameId);
656:       try {
657:         if (multitrackRef.current) {

659:           if (mt._layoutObserver) {

663:           wssList.forEach((ws: WaveSurfer) => {

667:               if (wsId === 'placeholder' || wsId === 'metronome') return;
668:               
669:               if (ws.isPlaying()) ws.pause();
670:             } catch {
671:               // ignore
672:             }
673:           });
674:           multitrackRef.current.destroy();
675:         }
676:       } catch (e) {

678:       }
679:     };
680:   }, [trackStructureHash, waveformQuality, secondsPerBar]);
681: 
682:   // Sync track heights dynamically
683:   useEffect(() => {
684:     if (!multitrackRef.current || !isReady) return;

686:     const expandedHeight = Math.floor(trackHeight * 1.5);

688:     const currentMetronomeHeight = metronomeHeight;

691:     let itemIndex = 0;

697:

699:       for (let j = 0; j < count; j++) {
700:         // Skip placeholder track in wssList

703:           if (wsId !== 'placeholder') break;

705:         }
706:         
707:         const ws = wssList[itemIndex];

715:       }
716:     });
717:     
718:     adjustLayout();
719:   }, [selectedTrackId, envelopeLocked, isReady, tracks, adjustLayout, trackHeight, metronomeHeight]);
720:

984:     const secondsPerBeat = 1 / beatsPerSecond;
985:     const secondsPerBar = secondsPerBeat * (debouncedTimeSignature?.[0] || 4);
986:     
987:     const targetDuration = Math.max(600, duration + 300);
988:     const targetBars = Math.ceil(targetDuration / secondsPerBar);
989:     const finalTargetDuration = targetBars * secondsPerBar;
990:     
991:     const metronomePhrases = (tracks || []).find(t => t.id === 'metronome')?.phrases || [];
992:     
993:     // Check if we need a completely new set of phrases (BPM or time signature changed)
994:     const needsNewPhrases = metronomePhrases.length === 0 ||

1000:     perfLogger.log(4);
1001:     
1002:     const fullBuffer = metronomeEngineRef.current!.generateClickTrackBuffer(
1003:       debouncedBpm,
1004:       debouncedTimeSignature,
1005:       finalTargetDuration
1006:     );
1007:     
1008:     metronomeBufferRef.current = { bpm: debouncedBpm, timeSignature: debouncedTimeSignature, buffer: fullBuffer };
1009:     
1010:     // Convert to WAV for WaveSurfer to render properly
1011:     const wav = audioBufferToWav(fullBuffer);
1012:     const blob = new Blob([wav], { type: 'audio/wav' });
1013:     const url = URL.createObjectURL(blob);
1014:     
1015:     const newPhrases = [{
1016:       id: 'metronome', // Consistent ID
1017:       url,
1018:       blob,
1019:       audioBuffer: fullBuffer,
1020:       startPosition: 0,
1021:       duration: fullBuffer.duration,
1022:       createdAt: Date.now(),
1023:       name: '',
1024:       color: '#718096',
1025:       volume: 1,
1026:       isMuted: false,
1027:       isSolo: false,
1028:       envelope: []
1029:     }];
1030:     
1031:     useStore.setState((state) => {

1044: 
1045:   const playPause = useCallback(async () => {

1047:

1050:       await audioContextRef.current.resume();
1051:     }
1052: 
1053:     if (useStore.getState().isRecording) {

1055:       if (recordingEngineRef.current) {

1058:         } else {
1059:           recordingEngineRef.current.stopRecording();
1060:         }
1061:       }
1062:     } else {

1064:       
1065:       // Start AudioWorklet early when going from stopped to playing
1066:       if (!wasPlayingRef.current && newPlayingState) {
1067:         console.log('playPause: transitioning from stopped to playing, initializing AudioWorklet early');
1068:         recordingEngineRef.current?.initializeForPlayback();
1069:       }
1070:       
1071:       // Stop AudioWorklet when going from playing to stopped
1072:       if (wasPlayingRef.current && !newPlayingState) {
1073:         console.log('playPause: transitioning from playing to stopped, stopping AudioWorklet');
1074:         recordingEngineRef.current?.stopForPlayback();
1075:       }
1076:       
1077:       wasPlayingRef.current = newPlayingState;
1078:       setIsPlaying(newPlayingState);
1079:     }
1080:   }, [isPlaying, setIsPlaying, setIsRecording]);
1081: 
1082:   const seekTo = useCallback((userTime: number, allowPreRoll = false) => {
1083:     if (!multitrackRef.current) return;
1084:     
1085:     const beatsPerSecond = (bpm || 120) / 60;
1086:     const secondsPerBeat = 1 / beatsPerSecond;
1087:     const secondsPerBar = secondsPerBeat * (timeSignature?.[0] || 4);
1088:     
1089:     // Manual seeking (from UI) should be clamped to 0.
1090:     // Internal seeking (like for recording pre-roll) can go negative down to one bar.
1091:     const finalUserTime = allowPreRoll ? Math.max(-secondsPerBar, userTime) : Math.max(0, userTime);
1092:     const realTime = finalUserTime + secondsPerBar;
1093: 
1094:     // Update store immediately for responsiveness
1095:     setCurrentTime(finalUserTime);
1096: 
1097:     try {
1098:       if (typeof multitrackRef.current.setTime === 'function') {
1099:         multitrackRef.current.setTime(realTime, true); 
1100:       } else {
1101:         // Fallback for older versions
1102:         const maxDuration = multitrackRef.current.maxDuration || 1;
1103:         multitrackRef.current.seekTo(realTime / maxDuration);
1104:       }
1105:     } catch (err) {
1106:       console.error('Seek error:', err);
1107:     }
1108:   }, [bpm, timeSignature, setCurrentTime]);
1109: 
1110:   const startRecording = useCallback(async (trackId: string) => {
1111:     if (!recordingEngineRef.current) {

1115:     await recordingEngineRef.current.startRecording(trackId);

1117:

1120:     recordingEngineRef.current.stopRecording();
```

Let me reconsider the correct approach:

**The original approach was:**
1. Worklet waits until `currentTime >= _targetStartReal` before setting `_isRecording = true`
2. `_targetStartReal = punchInUserTime_Real - 1.0` (1 second before the punch-in point)
3. This means the worklet starts recording 1 second before the punch-in, capturing audio into the rolling buffer
4. When recording actually starts, the rolling buffer already has 1 second of pre-roll audio

**The problem with the original approach:**
When AudioContext is suspended, `currentTime` doesn't advance, so the worklet never reaches the target time. This is the bug I was trying to fix.

**The correct fix:**
Instead of removing the waiting logic entirely, we should ensure the AudioContext is resumed BEFORE posting the START_RECORDING message. The `initAudioWorklet()` function already does `await audioContext.resume()`, but this happens once. If the context gets suspended again (e.g., when the tab loses focus), the audio time stalls.

When `_flush()` runs:
- `recordStartFrame = this._recordingStartFrame = 11392`
- For each chunk in rolling buffer:
  - If `chunkEndFrame <= recordStartFrame`: include as head
  - If `chunk.frame < recordStartFrame`: include partial
  - Else: break

So if the first chunk in the rolling buffer has frame = 11392 (or the chunk that contains frame 11392), then `chunkEndFrame > recordStartFrame` and `chunk.frame <= recordStartFrame`, so we'd take the partial. But if there are no chunks WITH frame < 11392, then all chunks have `frame >= recordStartFrame`, and we'd hit the `break` immediately, giving rollingOffset = 0.

This would happen if NO process() calls ran between when the mic was connected and when the START_RECORDING message was processed. That's unusual.

OR: the rolling buffer might have been filled with audio, but then trimmed. The trimming removes chunks where `currentFrame - chunk.frame > maxFrames` (88200 for 2 seconds). During the time between initialization and recording, all buffered data would be within the 2-second window, so it shouldn't be trimmed.

- So `_recordingStartFrame === currentFrame` of this first process() call

   - `_recordingStartFrame` = X
3. process() call for frame X+1 runs:
   - Audio pushed to rolling buffer (frame X+1)
   - Audio also pushed to _audioData (frame X+1, because _isRecording is now true)

3. The `anchorFrame` in the STOP message should be `_anchoredFrame` (computed from `punchInUserTime_Real`)

This way:
- The audio buffer contains: [rolling buffer data before recording start][recording data]
- `anchorFrame` tells us where the DEFINITIVE audio starts in the shared clock
- `startPosition = anchorFrame/sr - sPB - HL` puts the clip correctly on the timeline

The rolling buffer provides the "head" — audio that was playing during the count-in/pre-roll. If the AudioWorklet only captures mic input, the head will be ambient noise (or silence if in a quiet room). The count-in metronome clicks are in the playback stream, not the mic stream.

- Contains only mic audio from that point onward

But wait, the recording CAN'T start at the right time because the AudioWorklet needs time to start processing. The message is sent from the main thread and processed asynchronously by the worklet's audio thread.

In the OLD code, `_shouldStart` was set to true immediately, and recording began when `currentTime >= _targetStartReal`. The `_targetStartReal` was supposed to be `punchInUserTime_Real - 1.0` (one second before the punch-in), which would give the rolling buffer time to fill. BUT `_targetStartReal` was never assigned! So recording started immediately when the message was received.

**The REAL bug in the original code was the missing `_targetStartReal` assignment!**

### Complete fix plan:

1. **Worklet**: Fix `_flush()` to return `_anchoredFrame` (this is the sync anchor from punchInUserTime_Real)
2. **Worklet**: Add the missing `_targetStartReal` assignment and KEEP the delayed start mechanism
3. **Everything else**: The conversions already fixed in previous steps should be correct

Actually wait. I need to also think about what `_targetStartReal` should be set to. In the original design:
- `_targetStartReal = punchInUserTime_Real - 1.0` (1 second before punch-in = start of head/rolling buffer capture)
- But this should be configurable based on `headLength` (HL)
- `_targetStartReal = punchInUserTime_Real - HL`

But there's a subtlety: the rolling buffer size must be >= HL. Currently it's `max(2.0, HL + 1.0)`, which is at least 2 seconds. With HL default of 1.0, the buffer covers the 1 second before punch-in.

If we set `_targetStartReal = punchInUserTime_Real - HL`, then:
- Recording starts at `punchInUserTime - HL` seconds in RealTime
- The rolling buffer has been accumulating for HL seconds before recording starts
- These HL seconds of rolling buffer become the "head"
- Then the actual recording (after the head) contains the definitive audio from punchInUserTime onward

This is EXACTLY the intended behavior!

But we also need to handle the case where the AudioContext might be suspended. The fix for that: resume the AudioContext BEFORE waiting for the target start time, and use the worklet's internal clock (currentFrame/sampleRate) instead of audioContext.currentTime for comparison.

Wait, `currentTime` in the worklet IS `currentFrame / sampleRate`, which is the same as the shared clock. So the comparison `currentTime >= _targetStartReal` should work correctly as long as the AudioContext is running.

And the AudioContext resume is already handled in initAudioWorklet with `await audioContext.resume()`. The issue is that `resume()` is async and might not complete before startRecording() is called. But that's handled at the useAudioEngine level with the "fire-and-forget" approach.

5. `initAudioWorklet()` resumes the AudioContext (await)
6. Then recording begins

Since `initAudioWorklet()` already awaits the resume, the AudioContext SHOULD be running by the time we post START_RECORDING.

2. User presses Record while playing

This should work. The AudioContext should be running.

For the "stopped" case:
1. User is at UserTime > 0 (playhead was moved)
2. User presses Record
3. `initStream()` is called (new mic stream needed since previous might be closed)
4. `initAudioWorklet()` resumes AudioContext (awaited)
5. Then recording begins with seek to pre-roll position

But the AudioContext resume is async! It might not complete before the AudioWorklet starts processing. However, looking at the code:

```js
// In initAudioWorklet():
if (audioContext.state === 'suspended') {
    await audioContext.resume();
}
```

The `await` means the function won't return until the AudioContext is running. So by the time `startRecording()` continues after `await this.initStream()`, the AudioContext should be running.

But then, the post-message to the worklet happens synchronously after `initStream()` returns. And the worklet processes the message on the next audio thread quantum. Since the AudioContext is running, `currentTime` advances, and the delayed start mechanism should work.

Actually, let me re-examine one more thing. The RecordingEngine:

```js
let punchInUserTime_Real: number;
if (isCurrentlyPlaying) {
    punchInUserTime_Real = audioContext.currentTime;
} else {
    const startupDelay = 0.15;
    punchInUserTime_Real = audioContext.currentTime + startupDelay + timeFromPlaybackStartToPunchIn;
}
```

With the delayed start mechanism restored in the worklet, the `_targetStartReal` would be `punchInUserTime_Real - HL`. The worklet would wait until `currentTime >= _targetStartReal` before starting recording.

The `punchInUserTime_Real` sent by the main thread is the estimated RealTime when the punch-in should happen. It includes an estimated `startupDelay` of 0.15s. But since we now resume the AudioContext explicitly in initAudioWorklet(), and the `initStream()` call awaits the resume, the startupDelay might not be needed.

But actually, when `preRollMode = 'always'` and not currently playing:
- The code seeks to -2s (UserTime), which is RealTime 0
- Then starts playback
- Recording punches in 2 seconds later (when UserTime reaches 0)
- The recording message includes the estimated RealTime of the punch-in

The startup delay (0.15s) accounts for:
1. Time for `onSetIsPlaying` to actually start playback
2. Time for the first audio render quanta

With the delayed start in the worklet, even if playback starts slightly late, the worklet will wait until `currentTime >= _targetStartReal` before recording. So the punchInUserTime_Real calculation still needs to be reasonably accurate.

For the "currently playing" case:
- `punchInUserTime_Real = audioContext.currentTime`
- This assumes the punch-in happens AT the moment the user presses record AND the AudioContext time is accurate
- But there's a message delay between the main thread and the worklet
- The worklet uses `currentTime >= _targetStartReal = punchInUserTime_Real - HL`
- By the time the message arrives, `currentTime` might already be past `_targetStartReal`
- So recording starts immediately after message arrival — which is fine

For the "stopped" case:
- `punchInUserTime_Real = audioContext.currentTime + 0.15 + timeFromPlaybackStartToPunchIn`
- This tries to predict the future state of the AudioContext
- With the delayed start mechanism, even if the prediction is slightly off, the worklet will start at exactly `_targetStartReal`

Let me reconsider the entire time mapping.

**User Time ↔ Real Time mapping:**
- Real Time = User Time + secondsPerBar (for recording/playback)
- User Time 0 (Bar 1.1) = Real Time sPB

The seekTo function maps:
```
seekTo(userTime, allowPreRoll) {
    finalUserTime = max(-sPB, userTime)
    realTime = finalUserTime + sPB
    multitrack.setTime(realTime)
}
```

And multitrack internally uses RealTime:
```
updatePosition(time) { // time is RealTime
    newTime = time - track.startPosition  // newTime is time within the track's buffer
}
```

So `track.startPosition` is in RealTime. When a new track is created via initial construction:
```
realStartPosition = p.startPosition + sPB + offset  // Convert UserTime to RealTime
```

And for playback:
```
track.startPosition = RealTime - (RealTime - track.startPosition) = 0 at the track's start
```

When playback is at some RealTime `T`:
- The track plays audio from buffer position `T - track.startPosition`
- This means if `track.startPosition = -sPB`, the audio at RealTime 0 (UserTime -sPB) would be at buffer position `0 - (-sPB) = sPB`

Wait, the buffer for a recording starts at the beginning of the recorded audio. The buffer position 0 corresponds to `startPosition` in RealTime.

For audio to play at UserTime 0 (RealTime sPB):
- `newTime = RealTime(sPB) - track.startPosition`
- For newTime to be 0 at playback start, track.startPosition = sPB
- But the recording audio starts at buffer[0] which should be heard at track.startPosition

OK let me re-derive:

If `track.startPosition = p.startPosition + sPB` (converting UserTime to RealTime), and the recorded audio buffer starts at the punch-in point:

When RealTime = `track.startPosition`:
- `newTime = track.startPosition - track.startPosition = 0`
- The `WebAudioPlayer` plays from offset 0 in its buffer
- This means the first sample of the recorded audio is heard at RealTime = `track.startPosition`

For this to be correct:
- `track.startPosition` (RealTime) should be when the first sample of the audio should be heard

OK, let me just write the clearest implementation:

**Worklet:**
- On START_RECORDING: 
  - `_anchoredFrame = floor(punchInUserTime_Real * sampleRate)` 
  - `_recordingStartFrame = _anchoredFrame - floor(headLength * sampleRate)` (but not before `currentFrame`)
  - Set a flag but DON'T start recording into `_audioData` yet
- In process():
  - Always capture to rolling buffer
  - If `!_isRecording && currentFrame >= _recordingStartFrame`: set `_isRecording = true`, start `_audioData = []`
- In _flush():
  - Split rolling buffer at `_recordingStartFrame`
  - Return `[_combinedAudio, _anchoredFrame, _rollingOffset]`

This way:
- Rolling buffer captures audio from the start (pre-roll for "none" mode)
- Recording into `_audioData` starts `headLength` seconds after first audio arrives
- The flush combines rolling buffer head + recorded audio
- The anchor is the sync point returned to main thread

In RealTime: `anchorFrame/sr` is where definitive recording starts. The clip starts at `anchorFrame/sr - headLength` in RealTime.

So if `anchorFrame/sr` (RealTime) = UserTime 4.0 + secondsPerBar:

This means the clip VISUALLY starts at UserTime 3.0, with the head covering [3.0, 4.0] and the definitive recording covering [4.0, end]. The user pressed at UserTime 4.0, and the definitive recording starts there.

This is actually the intended behavior! The head is a "lookback" buffer. The clip starts `headLength` before the punch-in and the definitive recording begins at the punch-in point.

BUT for "none" mode, the head should contain meaningful audio (what was just played). Currently, because recording starts immediately, the rolling buffer only has a few frames, so the head is mostly silence or very early audio.

### Revised Plan

**Worklet:**

```javascript
// On START_RECORDING message:
_anchoredFrame = floor(punchInUserTime_Real * sampleRate)

// Delay recording start by headLength so the rolling buffer fills with pre-head audio
const headFrames = floor(headLength * sampleRate)
_recordingStartFrame = _anchoredFrame - headFrames  // Can't be negative in practice

// If _recordingStartFrame is in the future, set flag and wait
// If it's already past, start immediately
if (currentFrame >= _recordingStartFrame) {
  _isRecording = true
  _audioData = []
} else {
  _pendingStartFrame = _recordingStartFrame
}
```

In process():
```
// Always capture to rolling buffer
...

// Check if it's time to start recording into _audioData
if (_pendingStartFrame !== null && currentFrame >= _pendingStartFrame) {
  _pendingStartFrame = null
  _isRecording = true
  _audioData = []
}

if (_isRecording) {
  // capture to _audioData
}
```

In _flush():
```
// Split rolling buffer at _recordingStartFrame
// Return [_combinedAudio, _anchoredFrame, rollingOffset]
```

This ensures:
- "none" mode: ~1s of audio in rolling buffer before recording starts → meaningful head
- "always" mode: Pre-roll audio in rolling buffer → natural head from count-in bar
- `_anchoredFrame` returned in both RECORDING_STARTED and RECORDING_STOPPED messages
- Main thread uses `_anchoredFrame` for sync, not `_recordingStartFrame`

Wait, but there's a subtlety. In "none" mode:
- User presses Record, playback immediately starts
- `punchInUserTime_Real = audioCtx.currentTime + 0.15 + (punchInUserTime - recordStartUserTime)`
- Since `recordStartUserTime = punchInUserTime` for "none" mode:
  `punchInUserTime_Real = audioCtx.currentTime + 0.15`
  This is approximately right now + 0.15s.
  
- `_anchoredFrame = floor((audioCtx.currentTime + 0.15) * sampleRate)`
- `_recordingStartFrame = _anchoredFrame - headLength * sampleRate = roughly (currentTime - 0.85) * sampleRate`
- Since currentFrame corresponds to `audioCtx.currentTime`, and the message arrives a few frames after the current process(), `_recordingStartFrame` is slightly in the past.
- So recording starts immediately (no waiting).

The rolling buffer in this case only has a few frames. The head will be very short or empty.

Hmm. This is a fundamental timing issue with "none" mode. The rolling buffer only starts filling when the AudioWorklet process() begins running (when playback starts). By the time the START_RECORDING message arrives, it's too late to have captured head audio.

To solve this properly for "none" mode: recording into `_audioData` must be DELAYED by `headLength`. This means:
- AudioWorklet starts when playback starts
- Rolling buffer fills for `headLength` seconds
- THEN recording into `_audioData` starts
- The anchor point is `punchInUserTime_Real` (when the user conceptually "pressed record")
- The head covers `[punchInUserTime_Real - headLength, punchInUserTime_Real]` in RealTime

With the delayed recording start (`_recordingStartFrame`), the `_flush()` will split the rolling buffer to include audio from `_recordingStartFrame` to `_anchoredFrame` (the head), plus all of `_audioData` (the definitive recording).

BUT: The START_RECORDING message arrives almost immediately (within 0.15s of playback start). At that point, `currentFrame` is close to when playback started. We compute `_recordingStartFrame = _anchoredFrame - headLength * sampleRate`. Since `_anchoredFrame` corresponds to approximately `currentTime + 0.15`, we get `_recordingStartFrame ≈ (currentTime - headLength + 0.15) * sampleRate`. This is `headLength - 0.15` seconds in the past relative to current frame.

Since we can't go back in time, we need to start recording into `_audioData` from `currentFrame` but conceptually designate `_recordingStartFrame` as the split point for the rolling buffer.

Actually, I think the simpler approach is:
- For "none" mode: `_recordingStartFrame = currentFrame` (start recording now, as originally done)
- For "always" mode: `_recordingStartFrame = currentFrame - headLength * sampleRate` (conceptually go back and include pre-roll as head)

And the rolling buffer split always happens at `_recordingStartFrame`.

Wait, that's almost what the current code does. The issue was just that `_flush()` returned `_recordingStartFrame` instead of `_anchoredFrame`. Let me check: does the original pre-delayed code even work differently?

Actually, I think the current "immediate start" approach is OK for now, as long as `_flush()` returns the correct sync anchor. The head length will vary depending on how much was in the rolling buffer, but the sync will be correct.

This is because the rolling buffer in `_flush()` is split at `_recordingStartFrame = audioCtx.currentTime at recording start`. If recording started at RealTime 0.258 (because there was minimal delay), then the rolling buffer only has 0.258s of data before the recording. The head is 0.258 seconds, not 1 second.

**The real fix needs to ensure the rolling buffer has `headLength` seconds of data when recording starts.** This means recording into `_audioData` must be DELAYED by `headLength` in "none" mode.

### Final Corrected Approach

**Worklet changes:**

1. On START_RECORDING:
   - `_anchoredFrame = floor(punchInUserTime_Real * sampleRate)`
   - `_recordingStartFrame = Math.max(0, _anchoredFrame - floor(headLength * sampleRate))`
   - `_isRecording = false` (don't record yet)
   - `_awaitingStart = true` (flag to start recording when frame is reached)

2. In process():
   - Always capture to rolling buffer
   - If `_awaitingStart && currentFrame >= _recordingStartFrame`:
     - `_awaitingStart = false`
     - `_isRecording = true`
     - `_audioData = []`
   - If `_isRecording`: capture to `_audioData`

3. In _flush():
   - Split rolling buffer at `_recordingStartFrame`
   - Return `[combinedAudio, _anchoredFrame, rollingOffset]`

**RecordingEngine changes in startRecording():**

The `punchInUserTime_Real` calculation for "none" mode needs adjustment. Currently:
```
punchInUserTime_Real = audioCtx.currentTime + startupDelay + (punchInUserTime - recordStartUserTime)
```

For "none" mode: `recordStartUserTime = punchInUserTime`, so:
```
punchInUserTime_Real = audioCtx.currentTime + 0.15 + 0 = audioCtx.currentTime + 0.15
```

This is approximately correct — the punch-in happens ~0.15s from now. But we also need to subtract `headLength` from the anchor to get the recording start:
`_recordingStartFrame = _anchoredFrame - headLength * sampleRate`

This happens in the worklet, not the main thread. The main thread just sends `punchInUserTime_Real`, and the worklet derives everything from it.

For "always" mode: `recordStartUserTime = punchInUserTime - secondsPerBar`, so:
```
punchInUserTime_Real = audioCtx.currentTime + 0.15 + secondsPerBar
```

The worklet receives this and sets `_recordingStartFrame = _anchoredFrame - headLength * sampleRate`. Since the pre-roll has been playing for `secondsPerBar` seconds, the rolling buffer has approximately `secondsPerBar` seconds of audio. The head extraction from the rolling buffer will be `_recordingStartFrame` relative to the buffer data.

Actually, there's still a problem. Let me look at when `currentFrame` relative to the START_RECORDING message. The message goes from main thread to worklet via `postMessage`, which is asynchronous. By the time the worklet receives the message and sets `_recordingStartFrame = _anchoredFrame - headLength * sampleRate`, `currentFrame` in the worklet has advanced.

For "none" mode, if `punchInUserTime_Real ≈ audioCtx.currentTime + 0.15` and `_recordingStartFrame = (audioCtx.currentTime + 0.15) * sr - headLength * sr = (audioCtx.currentTime + 0.15 - 1.0) * sr = (audioCtx.currentTime - 0.85) * sr`, then `_recordingStartFrame` corresponds to a time ~0.85s ago. But `currentFrame` in the worklet corresponds to `audioCtx.currentTime` (plus message delay). So `_recordingStartFrame` is in the past and recording starts immediately.

This means the rolling buffer only has a few frames of audio (whatever was processed since playback started). The head will be very short.

**The fundamental problem is:** For "none" mode, we can't retroactively capture audio from before playback started. The rolling buffer only has data from when playback began.

One solution: **Delay the entire recording start** — don't begin playback and recording simultaneously. Instead:
1. Start playback
2. Wait `headLength` seconds
3. Send START_RECORDING message
4. Rolling buffer has `headLength` seconds of audio
5. Recording into `_audioData` captures from the anchor point

But this adds complexity to the main thread flow.

Alternatively: **The head is the last `headLength` of the rolling buffer, not the audio before recording starts.** Since the rolling buffer always captures (when playing), it contains the audio from playback start. When recording is triggered, the "head" is the most recent `headLength` seconds from the buffer.

In `_flush()`, the rolling buffer is split at `_recordingStartFrame`. If `_recordingStartFrame = _anchoredFrame - headLength*sr`, and recording into `_audioData` starts at `_recordingStartFrame`, then the rolling buffer data from `_recordingStartFrame` to `_anchoredFrame` IS the head.

But wait — `_recordingStartFrame` and `_anchoredFrame` are both in the past relative to `currentFrame`. The rolling buffer contains audio from various frames. The data between `_recordingStartFrame` and the start of `_audioData` capture IS the pre-roll head.

Let me trace "none" mode with the delayed approach:
1. t=0: Playback starts, AudioWorklet begins processing. Rolling buffer starts filling.
2. t=0 in main thread: START_RECORDING message sent with `punchInUserTime_Real ≈ 0.15`
3. Worklet receives message: `_anchoredFrame = floor(0.15 * sr) = 6615`, `_recordingStartFrame = 6615 - 44100 = -37485 → clamped to 0`
4. `currentFrame` in worklet is maybe 100 or so (a few ms of processing)
5. `_awaitingStart`: currentFrame < _recordingStartFrame? No (0 < currentFrame), so recording starts immediately
6. Rolling buffer has only a few frames of audio

So the head is essentially empty. This is the core problem.

**The solution: Delay the recording intentionally.** In the main thread, delay sending START_RECORDING by `headLength`:

In `RecordingEngine.startRecording()`:
```typescript
if (effectivePreRollMode === 'none') {
    // Delay recording start by headLength to fill the rolling buffer
    await new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, headLength * 1000));
}
```

But during this delay, playback is already running. The user might think nothing is happening.

Alternatively, handle it entirely in the worklet:
- On START_RECORDING, set `_recordingStartFrame = currentFrame + headLength * sampleRate`
- Set `_isRecording = false`, `_awaitingStart = true`
- In process(), check if `currentFrame >= _recordingStartFrame` before starting to capture to `_audioData`

This way:
1. User presses Record → START_RECORDING message arrives at currentFrame ≈ 100
2. `_anchoredFrame = floor(punchInUserTime_Real * sr)`, say 6615
3. `_recordingStartFrame = currentFrame + headLength * sr = 100 + 44100 = 44200`
4. Rolling buffer fills for ~1 second (44100 frames)
5. At frame 44200, `_isRecording = true`, `_audioData = []`
6. `_flush()` splits rolling buffer at frame 44200 → ~1s of head audio ✓

But now `_recordingStartFrame` (44200) ≠ `_anchoredFrame` (6615). They've diverged significantly. The `_flush()` returns `_anchoredFrame` (6615), but the actual recording data starts at frame 44200 (in the recording's frame space, this is frame 0).

The `rollingOffset` tracks how many frames of head audio precede the definitive recording. It's used for... what exactly?

Looking at how `handleAudioWorkletStop` uses these values:
```typescript
const anchorFrame = anchoredFrame ?? 0; // From STOP message
const startPos = (anchorFrame / sampleRate) - secondsPerBar - this.headLength;
```

So `anchoredFrame` from the STOP message is what drives the sync. As long as this is correct (= `_anchoredFrame` from the worklet), the `startPosition` will be correct.

The `rollingOffset` is currently unused in `handleAudioWorkletStop` (it's received but not used). It was apparently intended for something but isn't consumed.

So the fix is simpler than I thought:

**Worklet changes:**
1. On START_RECORDING: Set `_anchoredFrame` from `punchInUserTime_Real`. Delay the start of actual `_audioData` capture by `headLength` seconds.
2. In process(): Fill rolling buffer always. Only start `_audioData` capture after delay.
3. In `_flush()`: Return `_anchoredFrame` as the second element.

Let me also think about "always" mode. Currently:
- `punchInUserTime_Real ≈ audioCtx.currentTime + 0.15 + secondsPerBar`
- AudioWorklet has been processing for at least `secondsPerBar` seconds
- Rolling buffer has `secondsPerBar` seconds of audio

With the delayed recording approach:
- `_recordingStartFrame = currentFrame + headLength * sampleRate`
- Rolling buffer has `secondsPerBar + headLength` seconds by the time recording starts
- Head in the final audio = `secondsPerBar + headLength`? No, the head is only `headLength` seconds (split at `_recordingStartFrame`).

Wait, no. The `_flush()` splits the rolling buffer at `_recordingStartFrame`. Everything before `_recordingStartFrame` in the rolling buffer is head. If recording starts 1 second after the message, and the rolling buffer has 3 seconds of data (2s pre-roll + 1s delay), then `_recordingStartFrame` = currentFrame. Everything in the buffer before currentFrame would be head, which is the entire rolling buffer up to that point. That's 3 seconds.

But we only want `headLength` (1s) of head audio. The formula in the main thread uses:
`startPosition = anchorFrame/sr - secondsPerBar - headLength`

This accounts for `secondsPerBar` of pre-roll offset and `headLength` of head. The head audio in the buffer should represent the time from `startPosition` to the punch-in point, which is `secondsPerBar + headLength` total.

Hmm, this is getting complex. Let me step back and think about what determines the amount of head audio in the buffer.

The head audio = audio from `startPosition` (UserTime) to the punch-in point (UserTime).
Duration = `headLength` (by design).

In RealTime terms:
- Real start of clip = `startPosition + secondsPerBar` = `anchorFrame/sr - headLength`
- Punch-in = `anchorFrame/sr`
- Head = audio from `anchorFrame/sr - headLength` to `anchorFrame/sr`

So the head should be exactly `headLength` seconds. In the worklet, the head comes from the rolling buffer (audio before `_recordingStartFrame`). So we need:
- `_recordingStartFrame = _anchoredFrame - headLength * sampleRate`

For "always" mode: the rolling buffer has the pre-roll audio. If pre-roll started `secondsPerBar` before the punch-in, we have `secondsPerBar` seconds in the buffer. The `_recordingStartFrame` is `headLength * sampleRate` frames before `_anchoredFrame`. Since `secondsPerBar` might be greater or less than `headLength`, the buffer may have more or less audio than needed.

If `secondsPerBar >= headLength` (common: bar = 2s, head = 1s): The rolling buffer has more than enough. The split at `_recordingStartFrame` takes only the last `headLength` seconds, discarding the rest.

If `secondsPerBar < headLength`: The rolling buffer might not have enough. The head would be shorter than `headLength`.

In `_flush()`, the rolling buffer split:
```
for each chunk:
  if chunkEndFrame <= recordStartFrame:
    include entirely (head)
  elif chunk.frame < recordStartFrame:
    include partial (offset = recordStartFrame - chunk.frame)
    break
  else:
    break
```

So it takes all rolling buffer data up to `_recordingStartFrame`. If the buffer has 2 seconds and we split at 1 second in, we get 1 second of head. If we split at 2 seconds in, we get 2 seconds of head. The split point is controlled by `_recordingStartFrame`.

OK, let me set `_recordingStartFrame = _anchoredFrame - headLength * sampleRate`. As long as the rolling buffer has at least `headLength` seconds of audio before the anchor point, this works correctly.

For "always" mode: pre-roll = `secondsPerBar` seconds before punch-in. If `secondsPerBar >= headLength`, we're fine.

For "none" mode: We need to DELAY recording by `headLength`. This means:
- START_RECORDING arrives at `currentFrame ≈ X`
- `_anchoredFrame = floor(punchInUserTime_Real * sampleRate)` ≈ `X + small_delay`
- `_recordingStartFrame = _anchoredFrame - headLength * sampleRate`
- This is in the PAST (since `_anchoredFrame ≈ X` and we subtract `headLength * sampleRate`)
- `_recordingStartFrame < X` → recording starts immediately
- Rolling buffer only has ~0 seconds of data

So for "none" mode, we MUST delay recording intentionally. The worklet can't manufacture audio from the future.

**Approach for the worklet: Use `_recordingStartFrame` as the frame where recording into `_audioData` actually starts, and compute it as `currentFrame + headLength * sampleRate` for delayed modes.**

Actually, let me take a completely different approach. Let me align with what the user described:

> "the rolling buffer must be capturing from the moment it starts playback or if rec is pressed with count-in=none, which is the case where we start playback a headLength later, in order to have the head in the rolling buffer."

User wants: For "none" mode, playback starts, then recording kicks in after `headLength` seconds of rolling buffer accumulation. This is equivalent to the original code's `captureImmediately` behavior but with a delay.

Let me implement this in the RecordingEngine:

```typescript
// In startRecording, for "none" mode:
if (!isCurrentlyPlaying && effectivePreRollMode === 'none') {
    // Start playback first, wait headLength seconds to fill the rolling buffer,
    // then send START_RECORDING
    await new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, headLength * 1000));
}
```

And in the worklet:
```javascript
// On START_RECORDING:
this._anchoredFrame = Math.floor(punchInUserTime_Real * sampleRate);
// recording starts immediately (rolling buffer already has audio)
this._isRecording = true;
this._audioData = [];
this._recordingStartFrame = currentFrame;
```

And in `_flush()`:
```javascript
return [result, this._anchoredFrame, rollingOffset];
```

This way:
- "none" mode: Playback starts, rolling buffer fills for `headLength` seconds, then recording starts. Head = `headLength` seconds of audio.
- "always" mode: Pre-roll plays for `secondsPerBar`, recording starts at beginning of pre-roll. But wait, with no delay, the recording starts at `currentFrame` and the rolling buffer might not have much data yet...

Actually, for "always" mode the current code is fine — recording starts immediately and the rolling buffer has been accumulating since playback started (for `secondsPerBar` ≈ 2s). The head is whatever's in the rolling buffer before `_recordingStartFrame`.

Hmm, but in the current implementation, `_recordingStartFrame = currentFrame` (the frame when recording message arrives). The rolling buffer is split at this frame. For "always" mode, the buffer might have been accumulating for 2+ seconds before the START_RECORDING message arrives (since pre-roll playback started). So we'd get 2 seconds of head, even though `headLength` is 1 second.

To fix this, we should split the rolling buffer at `_recordingStartFrame = _anchoredFrame - floor(headLength * sampleRate)` instead of `currentFrame`.

### Final Implementation

**Worklet:**

On START_RECORDING:
```
_anchoredFrame = floor(punchInUserTime_Real * sampleRate)
_recordingStartFrame = max(0, _anchoredFrame - floor(headLength * sampleRate))
```

**RecordingEngine (startRecording):**

For "none" mode, delay the message:
```
if (!isCurrentlyPlaying && effectivePreRollMode === 'none') {
    // Wait headLength seconds for rolling buffer to fill
    await new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, headLength * 1000));
}
// Then send START_RECORDING
```

This way:
- "none" mode: Playback runs for `headLength` seconds, filling the rolling buffer. Then recording starts, capturing all subsequent audio. `_recordingStartFrame` is computed in the worklet as `_anchoredFrame - headLength*sr`, which is approximately `currentFrame` at that point. The rolling buffer has exactly `headLength` seconds of audio. ✓
- "always" mode: Recording starts immediately. `_recordingStartFrame = _anchoredFrame - headLength*sr` which is in the past (since `_anchoredFrame` ≈ currentFrame*sr). The rolling buffer has `secondsPerBar` seconds of data. If `secondsPerBar >= headLength`, we get exactly `headLength` seconds of head. If `secondsPerBar < headLength`, we get less (but this is an edge case). ✓

So `_recordingStartFrame ≈ (audioCtx.currentTime + 0.15 + secondsPerBar) * sr - headLength * sr = currentFrame + (0.15 + secondsPerBar - headLength) * sr`.

### Mental Model Reset

The "head" is a prefix of audio that precedes the definitive recording. Its purpose is to capture the count-in or the audio that was playing before the user pressed Record.

**How the head is captured:**
- The AudioWorklet runs continuously during playback
- It maintains a 2-second rolling buffer
- When recording starts, whatever is in the rolling buffer becomes the head

**For the head to be meaningful:**
- The rolling buffer needs to have been filling for at least `headLength` seconds before recording starts

**Current problem:**
- In "none" mode, recording starts almost immediately after playback begins → rolling buffer has <50ms of audio
- In "always" mode, pre-roll provides ~2 seconds of buffer before recording starts ✓

**Solutions:**

A) **Delay recording start in "none" mode** — Don't send START_RECORDING until `headLength` seconds after playback starts. This gives the buffer time to fill.

B) **Start playback `headLength` seconds BEFORE recording** — The app starts playback early, waits for buffer fill, then sends the recording start.

Option A is cleaner. But the `punchInUserTime` and related calculations need to account for the delay.

Actually, wait. The `punchInUserTime_Real` is computed in the main thread and sent to the worklet. If we delay the message, the main thread can recompute `punchInUserTime_Real` to account for the elapsed time, or we can adjust in the worklet.

But actually, `punchInUserTime_Real` represents "when in RealTime the definitive recording should appear". It's the sync anchor. It doesn't change with the delay — it's still "the moment in RealTime where the recording should be placed on the timeline".

So: we delay the actual start of audio capture, but we keep `punchInUserTime_Real` as the same sync anchor. The worklet just needs to know: "Start recording at frame X, but the sync anchor is at frame Y."

The delayed capture approach:
```
// Main thread sends immediately:
punchInUserTime_Real  // The sync anchor (when recording "conceptually" starts)

// Worklet receives and stores:
_anchoredFrame = floor(punchInUserTime_Real * sampleRate)

// Worklet delays actual capture by headLength:
_recordingStartFrame = currentFrame  // When recording physically starts
_captureAfterFrame = currentFrame + floor(headLength * sampleRate)
```

In process():
```
if (!_isRecording && currentFrame >= _captureAfterFrame) {
    _isRecording = true
    _audioData = []
}
```

In _flush():
```
// Split rolling buffer at _splitFrame = _anchoredFrame - headLength * sampleRate
// OR: _splitFrame = _recordingStartFrame (the frame where buffer accumulation began)
```

Hmm, but the rolling buffer split and the audioData capture need to align.

In _flush(), the head comes from: `rolling buffer data before _recordingStartFrame` + `_audioData` (everything recorded). 

`_recordingStartFrame` is set to `currentFrame` when the message arrives (or could be `_captureAfterFrame` — the frame where we actually START capturing audio).

Wait, I think the confusion is between what `_recordingStartFrame` means and what `_splitFrame` means in _flush().

Let me define clearly:
- `_recordingStartFrame`: The frame at which audio capture into `_audioData` starts. Used to split the rolling buffer.
- `_anchoredFrame`: The sync anchor frame (from `punchInUserTime_Real`). Returned to main thread.

The rolling buffer contains everything played before and during recording. In `_flush()`:
1. Take all rolling buffer chunks before or up to `_recordingStartFrame` → these become the "head"
2. Append all `_audioData` chunks → these are the "definitive recording"
3. Result = head + definitive recording

The head duration = (`_recordingStartFrame` - first frame in rolling buffer) / sampleRate.

For this to work with `headLength` seconds of head:
`_recordingStartFrame` should be set to `currentFrame` at the time the START_RECORDING message is received (or received + headLength delay).

The rolling buffer at that point contains audio from approximately `currentFrame - rollingBufferDuration * sampleRate` to `currentFrame`. That's ~2 seconds of audio.

If `_recordingStartFrame = currentFrame` and the rolling buffer has 2 seconds, the head will be ~2 seconds. But we want `headLength` seconds (default 1s).

So we should set `_recordingStartFrame` to be `headLength * sampleRate` frames BEFORE `currentFrame`. But that's in the past — those frames have already been processed.

**This is why the rolling buffer exists!** The rolling buffer stores the past audio, and we split it at the right point.

So:
1. `_splitFrame = _anchoredFrame - headLength * sampleRate`
   This is the frame in the shared clock where the head should start.
2. In `_flush()`, we split the rolling buffer at `_splitFrame`.
3. `_audioData` contains audio from when recording was enabled in `process()`.

For "none" mode (delayed capture):

- `_anchoredFrame` = Y (≈ X + small delta)
- `_splitFrame` = Y - headLength * sr (≈ X - 0.85 * sr, i.e., ~0.85s in the past)
- We set `_isRecording = true` at `_splitFrame + headLength * sr = _anchoredFrame` (which is ≈ X, essentially now)
- Wait, this means recording starts at `_anchoredFrame` which is NOW. But we want it to start `headLength` in the future.

### What the user wants for "none" mode:

`anchorFrame = 6.0 * sr = 264600`
`startPosition = 6.0 - 2.0 - 1.0 = 3.0` ✓

For this to work, the rolling buffer needs ~1 second of audio before recording starts. In the worklet:
- START_RECORDING arrives when playback has been running for ~0.15s
- Rolling buffer has ~0.15s of audio
- Need to wait ~0.85s more for a full 1s head

### OK, new simplified approach:

**Worklet stays mostly the same as my current code, but with these changes:**

1. On START_RECORDING message:
   - `_anchoredFrame = floor(punchInUserTime_Real * sampleRate)` (same as current)
   - Don't immediately set `_isRecording = true` and `_audioData = []`
   - Instead, set a target frame: `_captureStartFrame = currentFrame + floor(headLength * sampleRate)`

2. In process():

   - If `_awaitingCapture && currentFrame >= _captureStartFrame`:
     - `_awaitingCapture = false`
     - `_isRecording = true`
     - `_audioData = []`
     - `_recordingStartFrame = currentFrame` (mark where actual capture begins)
   - If `_isRecording`: capture to `_audioData`

3. In _flush():
   - `_splitFrame = _recordingStartFrame` (split rolling buffer at capture start point)
   - Return `[result, _anchoredFrame, rollingOffset]`

This gives:
- "none" mode: ~headLength seconds of rolling buffer captured as head ✓
- "always" mode: same, but rolling buffer already had secondsPerBar of audio before capture start

For "always" mode, `_recordingStartFrame` is set to `currentFrame` at the time the delayed capture begins. The rolling buffer might have 2+ seconds of audio. The split takes audio up to `_recordingStartFrame`, giving more than `headLength` of head. But the formula uses `headLength` in the `startPosition` calculation. If the actual head is longer than `headLength`, the formula still positions the clip correctly based on `_anchoredFrame` and `headLength`, even if the actual audio head is longer. The extra audio is simply unused.

In "always" mode:

- But `headLength` might be different from `secondsPerBar`

The formula `startPosition = anchorFrame/sr - secondsPerBar - headLength` places:
- anchor at punch-in RealTime
- start at `punchInRealTime - secondsPerBar - headLength`
- Since `punchInRealTime = UserTime + secondsPerBar`:

For the pre-roll recording case, the actual pre-roll audio goes from [-secondsPerBar, 0]. So if `headLength ≠ secondsPerBar`, the visual/head mismatch occurs.

But the user's headLength setting is meant to be the duration of pre-roll audio captured, and it's stored with the clip so SyncTool can adjust it. The startPosition formula always uses `headLength` regardless of actual pre-roll duration. This is by design — `headLength` is a per-clip metadata that represents how much head was captured.

### Implementation Summary

**Worklet changes:**

```javascript
// START_RECORDING handler:
_anchoredFrame = floor(punchInUserTime_Real * sampleRate)
_captureStartFrame = currentFrame + floor(headLength * sampleRate)  // DELAYED start
_awaitingCapture = true

// process():
if (_awaitingCapture && currentFrame >= _captureStartFrame) {
    _awaitingCapture = false
    _isRecording = true
    _audioData = []
    _recordingStartFrame = currentFrame
}

// _flush():
const returnFrame = _anchoredFrame  // NOT _recordingStartFrame
return [result, returnFrame, rollingOffset]
```

But wait, for "always" mode: `_captureStartFrame` is in the future. The pre-roll has been playing for `secondsPerBar` seconds. By the time we reach `_captureStartFrame`, there will be `secondsPerBar + headLength` seconds of rolling buffer. The split at `_recordingStartFrame` would give too much head.

Hmm, actually for "always" mode, the original behavior is correct — recording starts immediately (using `_recordingStartFrame = currentFrame`). The head is whatever's in the rolling buffer up to the recording start. The `startPosition` formula with `headLength` is an approximation.

So we need different behavior depending on `captureImmediately`:

**captureImmediately = true (none mode):**
- Delay recording by `headLength`
- `_recordingStartFrame` = currentFrame when recording actually starts
- This gives a proper head

**captureImmediately = false (always mode):**
- Record immediately (original behavior)
- `_recordingStartFrame` = currentFrame when recording starts (= message arrival time)
- Head = all rolling buffer before that point

```javascript
if (event.data.type === 'START_RECORDING') {
    this._shouldStop = false;
    if (event.data.punchInUserTime_Real !== undefined) {
        this._anchoredFrame = Math.floor(event.data.punchInUserTime_Real * sampleRate);
        const captureImmediately = event.data.captureImmediately || false;
        
        this._isRecording = false;
        this._audioData = [];
        
        if (captureImmediately) {
            // "none" mode: delay recording to fill rolling buffer with headLength of audio
            this._captureStartFrame = currentFrame + Math.floor(this._rollingBufferDuration * sampleRate * 0.5); // hmm this isn't right
        } else {
```
